proposals.title: Defining the Therapeutic Threshold: Quantifying the Optimal Fraction of Fibroblast-to-Cardiomyocyte Reprogramming for Functional Benefit Without Arrhythmic Risk
proposals.motivation: Direct cardiac reprogramming of fibroblasts to induced cardiomyocytes (iCMs) is a promising strategy for heart repair post-injury. However, pre-2026 studies focus on maximizing conversion efficiency, often assuming more iCMs are inherently better. Emerging clinical concerns suggest that incomplete or heterogeneous reprogramming within scar tissue may create a pro-arrhythmic substrate due to electrical heterogeneity and that excessive matrix remodeling could lead to detrimental tissue stiffening. A quantitative framework for an optimal, rather than maximal, conversion level is critically needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that there exists a defined, optimal range of fibroblast-to-iCM conversion efficiency that maximizes contractile recovery while minimizing electrical instability and adverse extracellular matrix remodeling, and that this optimum can be identified through integrated functional and electrophysiological metrics.
proposals.method: We will use a tamoxifen-inducible, cardiac fibroblast-specific Cas9 mouse model of myocardial infarction. Using AAV9 delivery of gRNAs targeting a safe-harbor locus for inducible expression of the reprogramming factors Gata4, Mef2c, and Tbx5 (GMT), we will titrate conversion efficiency via AAV dose and doxycycline-controlled gRNA expression. Outcomes will be assessed at 4- and 8-weeks post-MI using high-resolution epicardial mapping to quantify arrhythmia inducibility, pressure-volume catheterization for systolic/diastolic function, and second-harmonic generation microscopy to measure collagen alignment and tissue stiffness. Single-nucleus RNA-seq of the infarct border zone will correlate conversion fraction with transcriptional signatures of maturation and fibrosis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative 'therapeutic window' for cardiac reprogramming efficiency, directly linking a specific conversion threshold to integrated physiological outcomes. It will provide crucial pre-clinical design principles for future gene therapy trials, shifting the field's goal from maximal conversion to optimized, safe integration.
proposals.novelty: This proposal challenges the field's core assumption that more reprogramming is always better, introducing the novel concept of a 'sweet spot' for conversion defined by arrhythmic risk and tissue mechanics, not just contractile gain.

proposals.title: Spatially Targeted Reprogramming of Border Zone and Remote Cardiomyocytes via PHF7 Gene Editing for Post-Infarction Arrhythmia Mitigation
proposals.motivation: Current cardiac gene therapy and cell reprogramming strategies for myocardial infarction (MI) predominantly target the fibrotic infarct core, aiming to generate new contractile cells. However, this region is electrically inert and hostile to graft survival. The border zone, characterized by pathological remodeling and pro-arrhythmic substrates, and the remote myocardium, which undergoes compensatory hypertrophy, are largely neglected as direct therapeutic targets despite their critical roles in post-MI arrhythmogenesis and pump function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that targeted delivery of the pro-cardiomyogenic transcription factor PHF7 to the border zone or remote myocardium, rather than the infarct core, will yield superior functional and electrical integration, leading to greater improvements in contractile function and a more significant reduction in ventricular arrhythmias.
proposals.method: We will employ CRISPR/Cas9-based transcriptional activation (CRISPRa) for safe, transient PHF7 upregulation. Using a novel AAV9 serotype with a cTNT promoter for cardiomyocyte-specific delivery, we will perform spatially precise intramyocardial injections in a murine MI model to target: 1) the infarct core, 2) the peri-infarct border zone, and 3) the remote left ventricular free wall. Outcomes will be assessed via serial echocardiography, invasive hemodynamics, high-resolution optical mapping for action potential propagation and arrhythmia inducibility, and histology for fibrosis, hypertrophy, and connexin-43 gap junction distribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a paradigm shift in cardiac reprogramming by defining the optimal anatomical target for gene delivery to achieve functional benefit. It will provide the first direct comparison of infarct vs. non-infarct targeting on electrical stability, potentially identifying PHF7-mediated border zone reprogramming as a novel anti-arrhythmic strategy. The findings will critically inform the design of future clinical trials for heart failure gene therapy.
proposals.novelty: While PHF7's role in cardiomyocyte cell cycle re-entry is known, its application has been focused on scar regeneration. This proposal is novel in testing the non-obvious premise that modulating the electrophysiologically unstable border zone or the remodeling remote myocardium is more therapeutically valuable than directly addressing the scar.

proposals.title: Preclinical Safety and Efficacy of PHF7-Based Minimal Factor Cocktails for Direct Reprogramming to Smooth Muscle Cells
proposals.motivation: Smooth muscle dysfunction underlies devastating vascular and visceral diseases, but regenerative cell therapies are limited by the difficulty of generating functional, patient-specific smooth muscle cells (SMCs). Pre-2026 work by Olson and others established that the transcription factor PHF7 is a potent driver of SMC fate. However, translating this discovery requires moving beyond proof-of-concept to develop clinically viable, minimal-factor reprogramming strategies that minimize genomic instability and tumorigenic risk compared to traditional iPSC or multi-factor approaches.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a minimal, non-integrating cocktail centered on PHF7 can efficiently and safely reprogram human somatic cells into functional, stable SMCs suitable for autologous therapy in pre-clinical models of smooth muscle disease.
proposals.method: We will engineer non-integrating Sendai virus or mRNA vectors to deliver PHF7 alone or with 1-2 synergistic factors (e.g., MYOCD) into human dermal fibroblasts or blood-derived cells from healthy donors and patients with ACTA2-related smooth muscle dysfunction. Reprogramming efficiency and SMC purity will be assessed by flow cytometry for SMC markers (ACTA2, MYH11, CNN1) and functional assays (contractility, ECM production). Comprehensive pre-clinical safety will be evaluated by whole-genome sequencing for off-target effects, in vitro transformation assays, and in vivo teratoma formation studies in immunodeficient mice. The therapeutic potential of the resulting induced SMCs (iSMCs) will be tested in a humanized mouse model of ACTA2 vasculopathy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first pre-clinical safety profile for a PHF7-driven reprogramming therapy, directly informing regulatory pathways for clinical translation. It will generate a robust, scalable protocol for producing patient-specific iSMCs, providing a critical tool for disease modeling and a potential cell source for treating congenital and acquired smooth muscle disorders.
proposals.novelty: While PHF7's role in SMC specification is known, its application as the core of a minimal, clinically-oriented reprogramming cocktail for safety-focused pre-clinical development is novel and bridges a critical gap between basic discovery and therapeutic application.

proposals.title: A High-Content Screening Platform for Combinatorial Small Molecule Enhancement of PHF7-Mediated Smooth Muscle Cell Reprogramming
proposals.motivation: Direct reprogramming of fibroblasts to induced smooth muscle cells (iSMCs) offers a promising therapeutic strategy for vascular diseases and genetic smooth muscle disorders like ACTA2-related multisystemic smooth muscle dysfunction. The transcription factor PHF7 has been identified as a key driver of this reprogramming, but efficiency remains suboptimal for robust clinical translation. Pre-2026 work has demonstrated the power of small molecules to enhance reprogramming, yet systematic, high-throughput discovery of synergistic combinatorial regimens is lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a high-content, image-based screening platform using PHF7-driven reporter cells will identify specific small molecule combinations that significantly enhance the efficiency and fidelity of fibroblast-to-iSMC reprogramming.
proposals.method: We will engineer a human fibroblast line with a CRISPR/Cas9 knock-in of a fluorescent reporter (e.g., tdTomato) into the endogenous TAGLN (SM22α) locus, a canonical SMC marker. This line will be transduced with a doxycycline-inducible PHF7 construct. Using automated liquid handling, we will screen a library of ~1,000 small molecules, including epigenetic modifiers and signaling pathway agonists/antagonists, in pairwise combinations. High-content imaging (Opera Phenix) will quantify reporter activation, cell morphology, and co-staining for additional SMC markers (ACTA2, CNN1). Hit combinations will be validated by deep phenotyping, including RNA-seq and functional contractility assays.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first high-throughput platform for discovering reprogramming enhancers for iSMC generation. It will yield a validated set of synergistic small molecule cocktails that dramatically improve reprogramming yield and maturity. The platform and identified hits will serve as a critical resource for developing cell-based therapies for cardiovascular and smooth muscle pathologies.
proposals.novelty: While small molecule screens for cardiac or neuronal reprogramming exist, a dedicated high-content platform for smooth muscle reprogramming is novel. The focus on combinatorial screening, rather than single agents, is a non-obvious and powerful approach to uncover synergistic pathways that overcome the known inefficiency barriers of single-factor reprogramming.

proposals.title: Systematic Profiling of MyoAAV Tropism, Transduction Efficiency, and Immune Landscape in Human Cardiac Cell Types and Patient-Specific iPSC Models
proposals.motivation: Adeno-associated virus (AAV) vectors, particularly engineered MyoAAVs, are leading candidates for in vivo gene editing in cardiovascular diseases like Duchenne muscular dystrophy and ACTA2-related smooth muscle dysfunction. However, pre-2026 data indicate significant heterogeneity in AAV transduction across tissues and individuals, which could lead to incomplete therapeutic correction and variable efficacy. Furthermore, pre-existing humoral immunity and cell-type-specific innate immune responses to AAV capsids remain major safety and efficacy barriers for clinical translation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that MyoAAV transduction efficiency and the resultant immune activation profile are not uniform but vary significantly across distinct cardiac cell types (cardiomyocytes, fibroblasts, smooth muscle cells, endothelial cells) and are influenced by patient-specific genetic background and pre-existing immunity.
proposals.method: We will utilize patient-derived induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) from diverse genetic backgrounds, differentiated into the major cardiac cell types. Using a MyoAAV encoding a GFP reporter and a CRISPR/Cas9-based 'safe-harbor' targeting construct, we will quantify transduction efficiency (via flow cytometry and single-cell RNA-seq) and editing precision in each cell type. Concurrently, we will profile innate immune responses (e.g., interferon-stimulated gene expression) by bulk and single-cell transcriptomics and assess the impact of patient serum on transduction via in vitro neutralization assays. A novel computational pipeline will integrate these multi-omic datasets to identify cell-type-specific determinants of transduction and immune activation.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first comprehensive map of MyoAAV tropism and immunogenicity across human cardiac cell types within a patient-specific context. It will identify key barriers to uniform therapeutic gene delivery and define biomarkers predictive of adverse immune responses, directly informing the design of safer, more effective cardiovascular gene editing trials.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has characterized MyoAAV efficacy in whole muscle or animal models, a systematic, multi-omic analysis in a panel of *human* cardiac cell types from genetically diverse individuals, explicitly linking transduction data to immune readouts, has not been performed.

proposals.title: CRISPR-Cas9-Mediated CaMKIIδ Inhibition as a Universal Therapeutic Strategy in Diverse Cardiac Injury Models
proposals.motivation: CaMKIIδ is a well-established nodal signaling molecule in heart failure and ischemic injury, but its role in other cardiac pathologies, such as diabetic cardiomyopathy, chemotherapy-induced cardiotoxicity, or pressure overload without overt failure, is less clear and sometimes contradictory. Given the success of genomic editing in rescuing monogenic cardiovascular disorders in the Olson lab, applying this precise, durable strategy to modulate a central kinase like CaMKIIδ could reveal a common, targetable pathway across disparate injury etiologies, moving beyond symptomatic management to a potential universal disease-modifying therapy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CRISPR-Cas9-mediated disruption of the CaMKIIδ gene (or its phosphorylation/oxidation activation sites) will confer significant functional and structural protection not only in classic ischemic models but also in cardiac injury models where CaMKIIδ's role is considered minor or paradoxical, establishing it as a primary, non-redundant driver of pathological remodeling.
proposals.method: We will employ AAV9-delivered SaCas9 (or a high-fidelity variant) with gRNAs targeting exon regions or specific regulatory sites (e.g., Met281/282) of the CaMKIIδ gene in established mouse models: 1) chronic angiotensin II/phenylephrine infusion (neurohormonal stress), 2) doxorubicin-induced cardiotoxicity, and 3) high-fat-diet/streptozotocin diabetic cardiomyopathy. Control groups will receive AAV9 encoding a non-targeting gRNA. Outcomes will be assessed via serial echocardiography, invasive hemodynamics, histology for fibrosis and apoptosis, and molecular analyses of downstream CaMKII targets (RyR2, HDAC4, PLN) at 4-8 weeks post-injury.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively test the universality of CaMKIIδ as a master regulator of cardiac injury, moving from correlation to causation via permanent genetic inhibition. It will generate crucial pre-clinical data on the efficacy and safety of CaMKIIδ gene editing as a broad-spectrum therapeutic, potentially identifying a single target for multiple unmet clinical needs in cardiology.
proposals.novelty: While CaMKIIδ inhibitors have been tested pharmacologically, this proposal uses durable genomic editing—a method the Olson lab has pioneered for cardiovascular disease—to interrogate its role in understudied, non-ischemic injury paradigms where its primacy is disputed, offering a definitive genetic test of a central hypothesis in cardiac signaling.

proposals.title: In Vivo Adenine Base Editing of CAMK2D in a Porcine Model of Myocardial Infarction: A Preclinical Safety and Efficacy Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 work, including from the Olson lab, has established that base editing of the CaMKIIδ gene (CAMK2D) to correct its hyperactive oxidation-prone form (Met281/282Val) is a potent therapeutic strategy in mouse models, improving cardiac function post-injury. However, the translation of cardiac gene editing to the clinic requires demonstration of safety, biodistribution, and durable efficacy in a large animal model with human-like cardiac physiology and immune system. The pig is the established gold-standard for such preclinical cardiovascular studies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in vivo delivery of an adenine base editor (ABE) targeting the CAMK2D Met281/282 codons via a clinically relevant vector will be safe, achieve sufficient editing in cardiomyocytes of infarcted pig hearts, and lead to significant improvement in cardiac remodeling and function compared to control-treated animals.
proposals.method: We will employ a Yucatan minipig model of ischemia-reperfusion myocardial infarction. An ABE8e variant with high on-target efficiency, packaged into a cardiotropic AAV9 capsid (or a novel engineered capsid with high cardiac tropism, pre-2026), will be administered systemically post-injury. Editing efficiency and specificity will be quantified in cardiac tissue by deep sequencing (RhAmpSeq) and OFF-target analysis via unbiased methods like GUIDE-seq. Safety will be assessed via serial blood work, histopathology of major organs, and vector biodistribution. Cardiac function and remodeling will be serially evaluated by echocardiography and terminal pressure-volume catheterization, with molecular analysis of CaMKIIδ oxidation and downstream signaling.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive preclinical dataset on the safety and functional efficacy of CAMK2D base editing in a large animal. It will establish critical parameters for dose, delivery, and therapeutic window, directly informing the design of a first-in-human clinical trial. The work will also generate a generalizable framework for evaluating cardiac gene editing therapies in large models.
proposals.novelty: While base editing has been validated in mice and for ex vivo applications, its application to a large animal model of a common cardiac disease (post-MI remodeling) is novel. This proposal moves beyond proof-of-concept in genetically modified mice to address the translational challenges of scale, delivery, and immunology that are unique to large animals and humans.

proposals.title: In Vivo Base Editing of Smooth Muscle Actin to Reverse Pathological Remodeling in a Murine Model of Chronic Heart Failure
proposals.motivation: Chronic heart failure (CHF) is a leading cause of mortality, characterized by pathological cardiac remodeling and vascular dysfunction. While gene editing has shown promise in correcting monogenic defects in acute injury models, its efficacy in the complex, established milieu of CHF remains untested. This gap is critical because CHF involves maladaptive changes in cardiac and vascular smooth muscle cells that may not be reversible by strategies validated only in acute settings.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in vivo adenine base editing (ABE) of the pathogenic ACTA2 variant (R179H) in a pre-existing CHF model will rescue smooth muscle dysfunction, improve cardiac function, and reverse pathological vascular remodeling more effectively than interventions applied at the onset of injury.
proposals.method: We will employ a well-characterized mouse model of chronic heart failure induced by transverse aortic constriction (TAC) with 8-12 weeks of established dysfunction. At this chronic timepoint, mice will receive a single systemic administration of an AAV9-packaged ABE (e.g., ABE8e) and a single-guide RNA targeting the ACTA2-R179H mutation, identical to the editor used in our prior acute rescue studies. Cardiac function will be serially assessed via echocardiography and pressure-volume loops. Tissues will be analyzed for editing efficiency, smooth muscle contractility, fibrosis, and hypertrophy markers at endpoint.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first proof-of-concept for genomic base editing as a therapeutic intervention in established chronic heart failure, moving beyond prophylactic or acute correction. It will define the functional and molecular limits of reversibility for smooth muscle pathology in CHF and provide critical preclinical data on the safety and efficacy of editing in a chronically diseased cardiovascular system.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying a precision base-editing strategy not to prevent, but to reverse the sequelae of a chronic, multifactorial disease state, which represents a significantly more challenging and clinically relevant therapeutic window than acute injury models.

proposals.title: Synergistic Rescue of Heart Failure in a Humanized Mouse Model by Combinatorial CaMKIIδ Base Editing and SGLT2 Inhibitor Therapy
proposals.motivation: CaMKIIδ hyperactivity is a central driver of pathological cardiac remodeling and heart failure, but single-modality therapies often show limited efficacy. Pre-2026 work, including from the Olson lab, demonstrates that base editing can correct pathogenic variants and that SGLT2 inhibitors (SGLT2i) provide robust clinical cardioprotection, yet their mechanisms are distinct. Testing their combination is crucial to determine if additive or synergistic benefits can be achieved, potentially defining a new multi-pronged therapeutic strategy for genetic and acquired cardiomyopathies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that combinatorial therapy, using adenine base editing to genetically silence pathological CaMKIIδ activity alongside pharmacological SGLT2 inhibition, will produce synergistic improvements in cardiac function and remodeling in a pressure-overload heart failure model, surpassing the effects of either intervention alone.
proposals.method: We will employ a humanized mouse model with a constitutively active CaMKIIδ variant. Mice will undergo transverse aortic constriction (TAC) to induce heart failure and be randomized into four groups: control, AAV9-delivered CaMKIIδ-targeting base editor only, empagliflozin (SGLT2i) only, and combination therapy. Cardiac function will be serially assessed via echocardiography. Terminal analyses will include RNA-seq for pathway analysis, histology for fibrosis and hypertrophy, and assessment of mitochondrial function and calcium handling to delineate mechanistic interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish proof-of-concept for a novel combinatorial therapeutic paradigm merging precise genomic correction with metabolic modulation for heart failure. It will provide a mechanistic understanding of how editing a central nodal kinase interacts with SGLT2i pathways, identifying potential biomarkers for patient stratification. The findings could directly inform the design of future clinical trials for severe cardiovascular disease.
proposals.novelty: While both base editing and SGLT2 inhibitors have been studied individually in cardiology, their combination is unexplored. This proposal uniquely integrates a precise, durable genetic intervention targeting a specific disease driver with a systemic pharmacological agent known to improve clinical outcomes, testing for emergent synergistic properties rather than simple additive effects.

proposals.title: Cardiac-Targeted Lipid Nanoparticles for In Vivo Base Editing to Treat Hereditary Cardiomyopathy
proposals.motivation: Adeno-associated virus (AAV) vectors, the primary vehicle for in vivo gene editing, face significant limitations for cardiovascular applications, including pre-existing immunity, limited cargo capacity, and persistent transgene expression raising safety concerns. Lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) offer a promising non-viral alternative with high payload capacity, low immunogenicity, and transient expression, but pre-2026 cardiac-targeted LNP formulations for precise genome editing remain underdeveloped.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a novel cardiac-targeted LNP formulation can efficiently and safely deliver adenine base editor (ABE) mRNA and guide RNA to cardiomyocytes in vivo, enabling therapeutic correction of a pathogenic DMD mutation and restoring cardiac function in a humanized mouse model of Duchenne muscular dystrophy-associated cardiomyopathy.
proposals.method: We will screen a library of ionizable lipids and PEG-lipids to formulate LNPs encapsulating ABE8e mRNA and a gRNA targeting the common DMD exon 45 deletion mutation. LNPs will be functionalized with a cardiac-homing peptide (e.g., CTS) to enhance cardiac tropism. We will first assess editing efficiency and specificity in patient-derived induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-cardiomyocytes, then administer the lead formulation intravenously to our established humanized DMD mouse model. Cardiac editing efficiency, off-target effects, and functional outcomes (echocardiography, treadmill) will be evaluated over 12 weeks.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, non-viral delivery platform for cardiac gene editing, directly addressing the cargo and immunogenicity limitations of AAVs. It will provide proof-of-concept for a transient, redosable therapeutic strategy to correct monogenic cardiovascular diseases, expanding the toolkit for cardiovascular precision medicine.
proposals.novelty: While LNPs have been used for hepatic gene editing, the application of a peptide-targeted LNP for cardiac-specific delivery of a base editor represents a significant and unexplored advance, moving beyond proof-of-concept in the liver to address a critical organ delivery challenge.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Multiomic Profiling of Off-Target Effects in CRISPR-Edited Human Induced Pluripotent Stem Cell-Derived Cardiomyocytes
proposals.motivation: CRISPR-based genomic editing holds immense promise for treating cardiovascular diseases, including Duchenne muscular dystrophy and smooth muscle disorders, as demonstrated by Dr. Olson's recent work. However, the potential for off-target genomic alterations and their functional consequences in clinically relevant cell types, such as cardiomyocytes, remains a critical safety concern. Current bulk sequencing methods lack the resolution to detect off-target effects in heterogeneous cell populations and cannot link these events to resultant transcriptional and epigenetic dysregulation at the single-cell level.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that high-resolution single-cell multiomic profiling will reveal a spectrum of previously undetected, cell-type-specific off-target effects in edited cardiomyocytes, and that these genomic alterations will correlate with specific, deleterious changes in gene expression and chromatin accessibility.
proposals.method: We will perform CRISPR-Cas9 editing of a DMD or ACTA2 locus in human induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) and differentiate them into cardiomyocytes. Edited and unedited control cardiomyocytes will be subjected to parallel single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) using the 10x Genomics Chromium platform and single-cell ATAC sequencing (scATAC-seq). Data will be integrated using Seurat v4 and Signac pipelines to create unified multiomic profiles. We will identify potential off-target sites using GUIDE-seq in bulk populations and then search for their signatures (indels, aneuploidy) and associated transcriptional/epigenetic perturbations in the single-cell data.
proposals.contribution: This study will establish a comprehensive, high-resolution safety assessment framework for cardiac gene editing. It will provide the first detailed atlas of how off-target edits manifest functionally in a therapeutically relevant human cell type, identifying specific pathways vulnerable to disruption. The findings will directly inform the design of safer editing strategies and guide regulatory considerations for future cardiovascular gene therapies.
proposals.novelty: While off-target analysis is standard, applying integrated scRNA-seq and scATAC-seq to link off-target genomic events to their functional consequences in edited cardiomyocytes is novel. This approach moves beyond cataloging mutations to understanding their cell-specific phenotypic impact, a critical step for clinical translation not yet undertaken in the cardiovascular field.

proposals.title: Base Editing of a Pathogenic ACTA2 Variant in Human iPSC-Derived Cardiomyocytes Rescues Contractile Dysfunction Under Biomechanical and Metabolic Stress
proposals.motivation: Pathogenic variants in ACTA2 cause multisystemic smooth muscle dysfunction syndrome, including life-threatening thoracic aortic disease and coronary artery disease. While genomic editing rescued this in a mouse model, the efficacy and functional impact of correcting the human variant in the relevant human cell type—cardiomyocytes—under disease-relevant stress conditions remains unknown. Human induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived cardiomyocytes provide a critical, scalable platform to model this genetic cardiovascular pathology and test therapeutic editing strategies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that adenine base editor (ABE)-mediated correction of the pathogenic ACTA2 p.Arg179His (R179H) variant in human iPSC-derived cardiomyocytes will restore normal actin filament assembly and rescue contractile and calcium-handling deficits under biomechanical and metabolic stress.
proposals.method: We will generate iPSC lines from an ACTA2 R179H patient and an isogenic, corrected control using ABE8e delivered via electroporation. Cardiomyocytes will be differentiated using established small-molecule Wnt modulation protocols. Corrected and uncorrected cardiomyocytes will be subjected to pathological stress via cyclic mechanical stretch (10-15% elongation) and metabolic challenge (high lactate/low glucose media). Functional rescue will be assessed by quantifying contractility (video-based edge detection), calcium transients (GCaMP6f imaging), and sarcomeric organization (structured illumination microscopy of α-actinin and phalloidin staining).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a human iPSC-cardiomyocyte model for ACTA2 vasculopathy and provide the first direct functional evidence that base editing rescues cardiomyocyte-specific deficits. It will define the cellular pathophysiology of the R179H variant under stress and validate a precise therapeutic strategy, de-risking future clinical translation for this and related smooth muscle disorders.
proposals.novelty: While Olson's group showed rescue in vivo in mice, this proposal directly tests the editing strategy in the human genetic background and the specific cardiac cell type affected, under controlled pathological stress conditions not easily replicated in whole animals. This bridges the gap between in vivo proof-of-concept and human cellular therapeutic validation.

proposals.title: Controlled CaMKIIδ Activation via Gene Editing to Enhance Cardiac Physiological Adaptation in a Humanized Mouse Model
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 research has established CaMKIIδ as a central mediator of pathological cardiac remodeling in heart failure, with inhibition strategies being actively explored. However, its role in physiological adaptation, such as the beneficial hypertrophy and enhanced contractility seen in athlete's heart, remains poorly understood. This knowledge gap limits our ability to harness beneficial signaling pathways for therapeutic gain in chronic heart disease.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that controlled, cardiomyocyte-specific enhancement of CaMKIIδ activity will recapitulate key features of physiological cardiac adaptation, improving contractile function and metabolic efficiency without inducing pathological fibrosis or dysfunction.
proposals.method: We will use CRISPR/Cas9-based transcriptional activation (CRISPRa) in human induced pluripotent stem cell-derived cardiomyocytes (iPSC-CMs) to create a stable, gain-of-function CaMKIIδ model. A dCas9-VPR system with a cardiomyocyte-specific (e.g., TNNT2) promoter will be used to drive targeted overexpression of the endogenous CaMKIIδ gene. Parallel studies will employ AAV9 delivery of this CRISPRa system to adult cardiomyocytes in a novel humanized mouse model of pressure overload to test functional rescue. Phenotyping will include echocardiography, single-cell contractility assays, RNA-seq, and metabolomic profiling.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the specific molecular and functional outcomes of enhanced CaMKIIδ signaling, distinguishing its physiological from pathological roles. It will pioneer a novel gene-editing-based strategy for modulating kinase activity to promote beneficial cardiac remodeling, offering a new conceptual framework for treating heart failure.
proposals.novelty: While most studies focus on CaMKIIδ inhibition, this proposal directly tests the non-obvious hypothesis that its controlled activation can be therapeutic. It uniquely combines precise CRISPRa-based gain-of-function with a humanized disease model to interrogate a fundamental question in cardiac biology.

proposals.title: CRISPR-Cas9-Mediated Installation of a Constitutively Active CaMKIIδ Phosphomimetic Mutation to Model and Rescue Pathological Signaling in Induced Smooth Muscle Cells from Vascular Disease Models
proposals.motivation: Hyperphosphorylation of CaMKIIδ is a critical driver of pathological signaling in cardiovascular diseases, including heart failure and vascular smooth muscle dysfunction, leading to maladaptive gene expression and cell death. While transient chemical or genetic activation has been used, a stable, genomically integrated phosphomimetic model in human cells is lacking to dissect the chronic, extreme signaling states that drive disease progression. This gap limits our ability to test gene-editing strategies aimed at correcting such hyperactive signaling nodes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that introducing a stable, phosphomimetic mutation (e.g., T287D) in the endogenous CaMKIIδ gene in human induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived smooth muscle cells (iSMCs) will recapitulate extreme pathological signaling, and that subsequent allele-specific editing to revert this mutation will rescue downstream molecular and functional phenotypes.
proposals.method: We will use CRISPR-Cas9 homology-directed repair (HDR) to install the T287D mutation into the endogenous CAMK2D locus of control human iPSCs and iPSCs from patients with ACTA2-related smooth muscle dysfunction. Correctly edited clones will be differentiated into iSMCs. Pathological signaling will be assessed via phospho-substrate profiling, RNA-seq, and assays for calcium handling and contractility. A secondary editing step using a Cas9 variant (e.g., SpCas9-NG) with a patient-specific sgRNA will be designed to selectively revert the mutant allele in diseased iSMCs to the wild-type sequence, testing rescue.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, genetically precise human cellular model of chronic CaMKIIδ hyperactivation, providing a platform to identify its specific transcriptional targets in smooth muscle pathology. It will also pioneer a dual gene-editing strategy—first to create a disease mimic and then to correct it—demonstrating the potential of allele-specific editing to reverse acquired, signaling-driven disease states beyond monogenic loss-of-function disorders.
proposals.novelty: While CaMKIIδ's role is known, creating a stable phosphomimetic knock-in in human iSMCs to model a 'gain-of-phosphorylation' disease is novel. Furthermore, applying a two-step editing paradigm—install a pathological signaling mutation and then correct it—extends gene-editing therapy concepts to hyperactive signaling disorders, a significant advance beyond correcting null alleles.

proposals.title: Systematic Evaluation of Non-Viral and Novel Viral Vectors for In Vivo Gene Editing of Vascular Smooth Muscle Cells
proposals.motivation: While AAV9 is a standard vector for in vivo gene delivery, its use for smooth muscle cell (SMC) editing is limited by pre-existing immunity, limited cargo capacity, and potential genotoxicity from long-term episomal persistence. This is a critical barrier for developing durable therapies for SMC-driven diseases like ACTA2-related multisystemic smooth muscle dysfunction syndrome. Exploring alternative delivery platforms is essential for safer, more effective, and potentially curative cardiovascular gene editing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that engineered non-viral lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) or novel synthetic AAV capsids will enable more efficient, specific, and transient delivery of CRISPR-Cas9 ribonucleoproteins to vascular SMCs in vivo compared to standard AAV9 vectors.
proposals.method: We will screen a library of ionizable LNPs, formulated with SMC-targeting peptides, and a panel of engineered AAV capsids (e.g., Anc80, AAV-DJ variants) for their ability to deliver Cre recombinase or SaCas9/sgRNA ribonucleoproteins to SMCs in a Rosa26-tdTomato reporter mouse. The top candidates will be evaluated in a humanized ACTA2R179H mouse model of smooth muscle dysfunction, delivering CRISPR-Cas9 components to correct the pathogenic variant. Efficiency will be quantified by indel frequency (deep sequencing), protein rescue (immunohistochemistry), and functional rescue of aortic contractility and survival.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a comparative framework for SMC-targeted delivery, identifying a superior vector candidate for in vivo SMC gene editing. It will provide critical pre-clinical data on the safety and efficacy of non-viral delivery for correcting monogenic SMC disorders, moving the field beyond AAV dependency.
proposals.novelty: While AAV9 optimization is common, a head-to-head comparison of state-of-the-art LNPs and synthetic AAVs for SMC-specific RNP delivery in a therapeutically relevant disease model is lacking. This directly tests the assumption that AAV9 is optimal for this critical cell type.

proposals.title: Cell-Type Specificity of CRISPR-Cas9 Rescue in a Humanized Mouse Model of ACTA2-Related Multisystemic Smooth Muscle Dysfunction Syndrome
proposals.motivation: Genomic editing of the pathogenic ACTA2 variant in vivo has shown remarkable rescue of the multisystemic phenotype in mice, but the cellular mechanism of rescue remains unclear. It is unknown whether editing must occur directly in mature smooth muscle cells (SMCs), in SMC progenitors, or even in other cell types via systemic delivery and cross-correction. Determining the requisite cellular target is critical for designing safe and effective clinical gene editing strategies for vascular smooth muscle disorders.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that direct, efficient editing of the mutant ACTA2 allele in mature, differentiated smooth muscle cells is necessary and sufficient for phenotypic rescue, and that editing of progenitor cells or non-SMC lineages will be insufficient.
proposals.method: We will employ our established humanized ACTA2-R179H mouse model and cell-type-specific, Cre-dependent Cas9 expression systems. Using AAV9 vectors with smooth muscle-specific (e.g., Tagln-Cre) or progenitor-targeting (e.g., Pdgfrb-Cre) promoters to drive spCas9 and a corrective sgRNA, we will compare phenotypic rescue. Systemic, non-cell-type-specific delivery (e.g., Alb-Cre) will test cross-correction. Rescue will be quantified by histology (aortic wall architecture), vascular reactivity assays, and survival analysis, with editing efficiency assessed by targeted deep sequencing of FACS-isolated SMCs, progenitors, and hepatocytes.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the fundamental cellular requirements for successful in vivo gene editing of a dominant smooth muscle disorder, moving beyond proof-of-concept to mechanistic insight. It will directly inform the design of delivery vectors and regulatory elements for future cardiovascular gene therapies, prioritizing strategies that maximize on-target editing in the relevant differentiated cell type.
proposals.novelty: While previous work demonstrated that editing can rescue the phenotype, this study uniquely interrogates *where* editing must occur in the cellular hierarchy of the vessel wall, a question not addressed by prior systemic editing approaches. This shifts the focus from efficacy to mechanism, a critical step for clinical translation.

proposals.title: Genetic Modifiers of Multisystemic Smooth Muscle Dysfunction Syndrome: Interrogating the Penetrance and Severity of the ACTA2 R179H Variant
proposals.motivation: The ACTA2 R179H variant is a dominant, recurrent cause of Multisystemic Smooth Muscle Dysfunction Syndrome (MSMDS), a severe vascular disease. However, clinical heterogeneity among patients suggests that genetic background may influence disease severity, a concept unexplored for this variant. Understanding such modifiers is critical for accurate prognosis and for developing personalized therapeutic strategies beyond direct gene correction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the phenotypic severity of the ACTA2 R179H variant is modulated by genetic background and specific modifier genes, which can be identified through comparative analysis of isogenic models with differing genetic contexts.
proposals.method: We will use CRISPR/Cas9 to introduce the R179H variant into induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) derived from multiple healthy donors with diverse genetic backgrounds. These iPSCs will be differentiated into vascular smooth muscle cells (VSMCs) using established protocols. Phenotypic severity will be assessed via high-content imaging of contractility, cytoskeletal organization, and proliferation. We will perform RNA-sequencing on isogenic R179H vs. wild-type VSMC pairs from each donor to identify differentially expressed genes and pathways correlating with phenotypic severity. Candidate modifier genes will be validated via siRNA knockdown or CRISPRa/i in severe vs. mild background models.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational model for understanding variable expressivity in MSMDS, moving beyond the variant's sole pathogenicity. It will generate the first pre-clinical catalog of potential genetic modifiers for ACTA2-related disease, identifying new targets for adjunct therapies. The findings will refine genetic counseling by providing a framework to assess individual risk beyond the primary mutation.
proposals.novelty: While prior work by Olson's group rescued MSMDS in a single mouse model, this study uniquely focuses on human genetic diversity to explain clinical heterogeneity, a critical unanswered question. It combines isogenic editing with multi-donor iPSC models—a powerful but previously unexplored approach for this specific vascular smooth muscle pathology.

proposals.title: Systematic Optimization of AAV-CRISPR Vectors for High-Efficiency, Tissue-Specific Gene Editing in Porcine Models of Cardiovascular Disease
proposals.motivation: While CRISPR-based gene editing has shown remarkable therapeutic promise in preclinical models for muscular dystrophies and vascular smooth muscle disorders, clinical translation is bottlenecked by inefficient and non-specific delivery in large, human-sized tissues. Current AAV vectors, derived from small animal studies, exhibit suboptimal tropism and transgene expression in larger animals, limiting editing efficiency and raising safety concerns from off-target organ exposure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a combinatorial screen of novel engineered AAV capsids and tissue-specific promoters in a porcine model will identify a delivery vector that achieves significantly higher editing efficiency and specificity in cardiac and vascular smooth muscle compared to current standard serotypes.
proposals.method: We will employ a Cre-recombination-based AAV capsid screening platform (AAV9-based library) in a novel transgenic reporter pig to identify variants with enhanced cardiac and vascular tropism. Top candidates will be packaged with a SaCas9/gRNA construct targeting a safe-harbor locus and paired with a panel of muscle-specific promoters (e.g., cTnT, SM22α, MHCK7). Editing efficiency, biodistribution, and specificity will be quantitatively assessed in juvenile pigs via ddPCR of tissue DNA, RNA-seq of off-target tissues, and immunohistochemistry.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a rigorously validated, optimized AAV-CRISPR vector system for large-animal cardiovascular gene editing. It will establish critical efficacy and biodistribution benchmarks necessary for IND-enabling studies, directly accelerating the translation of editing therapies for conditions like Duchenne muscular dystrophy and ACTA2-related smooth muscle dysfunction syndrome.
proposals.novelty: This approach moves beyond testing single, naturally occurring AAV serotypes by performing direct in vivo selection of engineered capsids in a physiologically and immunologically relevant large animal model, coupled with a systematic promoter comparison, to solve the dual challenges of efficiency and specificity.

proposals.title: Tissue-Specific Promoter Selection for CRISPR/Cas9 Gene Editing: A Comparative Analysis of Efficacy and Off-Target Effects in Smooth Muscle Cell Phenotype Correction
proposals.motivation: Constitutive promoters like CMV drive high, ubiquitous expression of gene editors, which can increase off-target editing risks and immune responses, limiting therapeutic safety. For vascular diseases like ACTA2-related multisystemic smooth muscle dysfunction syndrome, restricting editor expression to smooth muscle cells (SMCs) could enhance specificity. A direct, quantitative comparison of SMC-specific versus constitutive promoter strategies in relevant models is needed to inform clinical translation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using an SMC-specific promoter (e.g., TAGLN/SM22α or MYH11) to drive CRISPR/Cas9 expression will achieve sufficient on-target editing to rescue disease phenotypes in patient-derived SMCs and mouse models, while significantly reducing off-target editing and immune activation compared to a CMV-driven system.
proposals.method: We will generate AAV vectors encoding SaCas9 and gRNAs targeting the pathogenic human ACTA2 variant, driven by either a CMV or an SMC-specific promoter. Using patient-derived induced SMCs (iSMCs) from ACTA2 mutation carriers, we will quantify on-target editing efficiency (via next-generation sequencing), phenotypic rescue (actin filament assembly, contractility), and genome-wide off-target effects (via GUIDE-seq). Parallel studies will be conducted in our established humanized ACTA2 mouse model, assessing vascular rescue, biodistribution of editor expression, and immune profiling.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, side-by-side evaluation of promoter strategies for in vivo gene editing, directly linking promoter choice to safety and efficacy metrics. It will establish best-practice guidelines for tissue-specific targeting in cardiovascular and smooth muscle disorders, moving the field beyond proof-of-concept towards safer therapeutic design.
proposals.novelty: While tissue-specific promoters are conceptually favored, a systematic, quantitative comparison of their performance against strong constitutive promoters in the context of correcting a precise, disease-relevant mutation in SMCs has not been performed, particularly with a focus on comprehensive off-target and immune profiling.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Transcriptomic Profiling of In Vivo CRISPR-Cas9 Editing Reveals Cell-Type-Specific Rescue and Off-Target Effects in a Humanized Mouse Model of ACTA2-Related Smooth Muscle Dysfunction
proposals.motivation: CRISPR-based genomic editing shows immense promise for treating monogenic cardiovascular and muscular diseases, such as ACTA2-related multisystemic smooth muscle dysfunction. However, current validation relies on bulk tissue assays, which mask critical heterogeneity in editing efficiency, transcriptional rescue, and potential off-target effects across distinct cell types within complex tissues like the aorta and heart. A cell-type-specific understanding is essential for predicting therapeutic efficacy and safety.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in vivo CRISPR-Cas9 correction of the pathogenic ACTA2 variant will exhibit cell-type-specific efficiency within the vascular wall and myocardium, leading to a transcriptional rescue signature primarily in smooth muscle cells, but may also induce distinct, unintended transcriptomic perturbations in other cell populations.
proposals.method: We will perform single-nucleus RNA sequencing (snRNA-seq) on aortic and cardiac tissue from our established humanized ACTA2 mutant mouse model following in vivo AAV9-delivered base editing. Tissues from edited, sham-treated, and wild-type mice (n=5/group) will be processed using the 10x Genomics Chromium platform. Data will be analyzed using the Seurat pipeline for clustering, cell-type annotation, and differential expression. Editing efficiency per cell type will be quantified by aligning sequencing reads to the target locus, and transcriptomic changes will be assessed for pathway enrichment using pre-2026 tools like AUCell and Gene Set Enrichment Analysis.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first high-resolution map of cell-type-specific outcomes for cardiovascular gene editing in vivo. It will define the precise cellular targets of rescue, identify any vulnerable cell types to unintended effects, and establish a robust single-cell omics framework for validating future editing therapies for muscular dystrophies and other inherited cardiovascular disorders.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Olson's lab has pioneered in vivo correction of ACTA2 mutations, applying single-cell omics to this model is novel, moving beyond gross functional rescue to dissect the heterogeneous cellular response, which has not been done for smooth muscle pathologies. This integrates cutting-edge pre-2026 single-cell technology with established gene-editing disease models.

proposals.title: CRISPR-Cas9-Mediated Correction of Pathogenic Variants in MYH11 and MYLK Rescues Smooth Muscle Dysfunction in Patient-Derived iPSCs and Engineered Vascular Tissues
proposals.motivation: Beyond ACTA2, pathogenic variants in other smooth muscle cytoskeletal genes, such as MYH11 (myosin heavy chain) and MYLK (myosin light chain kinase), cause severe aortopathies and vascular dysfunction with high mortality. Current management is limited to surveillance and surgical intervention, lacking disease-modifying therapies. A platform to correct these variants would validate a common therapeutic strategy for a class of related disorders.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the same CRISPR-Cas9-based editing platform successfully applied to ACTA2 can be adapted to precisely correct pathogenic variants in MYH11 and MYLK, thereby rescuing contractile function and cytoskeletal integrity in patient-derived smooth muscle cells.
proposals.method: We will use patient-derived induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) harboring known pathogenic MYH11 and MYLK variants. Using optimized CRISPR-Cas9 ribonucleoprotein (RNP) electroporation protocols from our prior work, we will perform homology-directed repair with single-stranded oligodeoxynucleotide (ssODN) donors. Edited clones will be validated by Sanger and deep sequencing, differentiated into smooth muscle cells (iSMCs), and assessed for contractile function, calcium signaling, and cytoskeletal organization in 3D engineered vascular tissue constructs.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a scalable gene-editing platform for multiple smooth muscle cytoskeletopathies, moving beyond single-gene proof-of-concept. It will provide critical pre-clinical data on functional rescue for MYH11 and MYLK variants and identify common versus gene-specific pathological mechanisms in edited iSMCs.
proposals.novelty: While editing ACTA2 is established, applying this precise platform to the larger, more complex MYH11 gene and the signaling kinase MYLK to correct specific pathogenic variants in patient-derived models is unexplored and tests the generality of the approach.

proposals.title: Development of a Myogenin-Targeting Antisense Oligonucleotide to Attenuate Smooth Muscle Hyperplasia in Vascular Disease
proposals.motivation: Pathological smooth muscle cell (SMC) proliferation is a central driver of occlusive vascular diseases, including restenosis after angioplasty and pulmonary arterial hypertension. Pre-2026 studies, including from the Olson lab, have shown that shRNA-mediated knockdown of the myogenic transcription factor myogenin (MYOG) can inhibit SMC proliferation and neointima formation in rodent models. However, shRNA-based approaches are not readily translatable to clinical therapy, creating a critical need for a pharmacologically viable myogenin inhibitor.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systemically deliverable, chemically modified antisense oligonucleotide (ASO) targeting MYOG mRNA will effectively reduce MYOG expression, inhibit pathological SMC proliferation, and attenuate vascular remodeling in pre-clinical disease models.
proposals.method: We will design and screen a panel of 2'-O-methoxyethyl (MOE) gapmer ASOs targeting murine and human MYOG transcripts. Lead candidates will be validated for potency and specificity in vitro using patient-derived SMCs harboring pathogenic ACTA2 variants, a model of hyperproliferative vasculopathy. The optimal ASO will be evaluated in two in vivo models: the wire-injury-induced carotid artery model in a humanized ACTA2 mouse and the monocrotaline-induced pulmonary hypertension model. Efficacy will be assessed by histomorphometry, hemodynamic measurements, and single-nucleus RNA sequencing of vascular tissue to define the transcriptional consequences of MYOG inhibition.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a first-in-class, translation-ready therapeutic modality targeting myogenin for vascular proliferative disorders. It will provide a comprehensive pre-clinical dataset on the efficacy and molecular mechanism of MYOG ASO therapy, bridging a critical gap between foundational shRNA studies and potential clinical application. Furthermore, it will validate myogenin as a druggable master regulator of pathological SMC state transitions.
proposals.novelty: While MYOG's role in SMC pathology is established, its targeting with a systemically deliverable, clinically relevant ASO platform is novel. This approach moves beyond proof-of-concept genetic knockdown to a therapeutic strategy directly informed by the lab's prior success in editing ACTA2 to rescue vascular dysfunction.

proposals.title: Identification of a Tumor-Derived Secreted Factor Inducing a Denervation-Like Gene Program in Skeletal Muscle via CRISPR-Based Functional Screening
proposals.motivation: Cancer-associated cachexia is a devastating syndrome of muscle wasting that contributes significantly to mortality, but its mechanisms are incompletely understood. Pre-2026 studies, including from our lab, have shown that skeletal muscle in cachexia exhibits a gene expression signature reminiscent of denervation, but the tumor-derived signal initiating this program is unknown. Identifying this factor is a critical first step toward developing targeted therapies to preserve muscle mass and function in cancer patients.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a specific secreted factor produced by cachexia-inducing tumors directly activates a denervation-like transcriptional program in skeletal muscle cells, and that this factor can be identified through a functional genomic screen of tumor secretomes.
proposals.method: We will employ a CRISPR/Cas9-based loss-of-function screen in a cachexia-inducing tumor cell line (e.g., C26 colon carcinoma) targeting all known secreted proteins and receptors. Conditioned media from individual knockout pools will be applied to cultured primary human or mouse myotubes. A luciferase reporter driven by key denervation-responsive enhancer elements (e.g., from the Chrna1 or Musk genes) will serve as the primary readout. Hits will be validated by recombinant protein addition and antibody-mediated neutralization assays, followed by in vivo testing in tumor-bearing mice using AAV-mediated muscle gene editing to disrupt the candidate factor's receptor.
proposals.contribution: This work will pinpoint a direct molecular link between tumor secretion and muscle transcriptional reprogramming, filling a major gap in the pathophysiology of cancer cachexia. It will provide a novel therapeutic target for blocking muscle wasting. Furthermore, it will establish a CRISPR-based screening platform for identifying systemic mediators of tissue-tissue communication in disease.
proposals.novelty: While denervation signatures in cachexia are recognized, no study has yet systematically screened for the tumor-derived effector responsible. Applying a direct functional genomic screen of the tumor secretome to a muscle-specific denervation reporter is a novel, unbiased approach to identify this elusive factor.

proposals.title: Characterizing Immune and Regenerative Deficits in a Humanized DMD Mouse Model for Therapeutic Translation
proposals.motivation: Humanized mouse models with patient-specific mutations, like the new Duchenne Muscular Dystrophy (DMD) model from the Olson lab, are crucial for pre-clinical testing of gene editing therapies. However, pre-2026 knowledge indicates that standard immunodeficient hosts (e.g., NSG mice) used for human cell engraftment lack a fully functional human immune system and may not accurately model the inflammatory microenvironment or immune-mediated clearance of corrected cells seen in patients. Furthermore, the regenerative capacity of human muscle stem cells in a mouse niche remains poorly characterized, creating a critical gap in predicting therapeutic durability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the current humanized DMD mouse model fails to fully recapitulate key pathological features, specifically the human inflammatory response to muscle damage and the authentic regenerative dynamics of human muscle stem cells, thereby limiting its predictive value for clinical outcomes.
proposals.method: We will use the established humanized DMD mouse model (hDMD-NSG) harboring a common patient mutation in the human DMD gene. First, we will engraft these mice with a matched human hematopoietic stem cell (HSC) population to create a humanized immune system (HIS). We will then compare the immune infiltrate (via flow cytometry for human immune cell markers) and cytokine profiles (via Luminex array) in damaged muscle of HIS-hDMD mice versus non-HIS controls. Second, we will employ in vivo lineage tracing of transplanted, CRISPR/Cas9-corrected human muscle progenitor cells using a lentiviral barcoding system to quantify their clonal expansion, differentiation, and long-term persistence following injury.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the specific immunological and regenerative limitations of a leading humanized DMD model. It will provide a framework for validating future humanized models of muscular dystrophy and smooth muscle disorders (e.g., ACTA2-related). The findings will directly inform the design of more predictive pre-clinical studies for cell and gene editing therapies by identifying which disease facets are accurately modeled.
proposals.novelty: While humanized models exist, a systematic evaluation of the human immune response and the clonal dynamics of corrected human muscle stem cells within the context of a precise DMD gene-editing model has not been performed. This integrates human immune system reconstitution with high-resolution lineage tracing to dissect model fidelity beyond simple genetic correction.

proposals.title: Longitudinal Assessment of Cardiac Structure and Function Following CRISPR-Cas9-Mediated Dystrophin Restoration in a Humanized DMD Mouse Model
proposals.motivation: Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) leads to severe, progressive cardiomyopathy, a primary cause of death. While CRISPR-based editing can restore dystrophin in skeletal muscle and heart tissue, the detailed temporal dynamics of cardiac functional recovery and structural remodeling post-correction remain poorly characterized. A comprehensive longitudinal analysis is critical to define the therapeutic window and the extent of functional rescue achievable with genomic editing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CRISPR-Cas9-mediated restoration of dystrophin expression in the myocardium will lead to a time-dependent, progressive improvement in cardiac contractile function and a reversal of pathological fibrotic remodeling in a humanized DMD mouse model.
proposals.method: We will utilize the previously established humanized DMD mouse model harboring a common patient mutation. Following systemic delivery of AAV9-CRISPR-Cas9 components at 6 weeks of age, cohorts will undergo serial, non-invasive cardiac phenotyping every 4 weeks until 1 year. This will include high-frequency echocardiography (Vevo 3100) for systolic/diastolic function and speckle-tracking strain analysis for myocardial deformation. Terminal histological and molecular analyses at defined timepoints will quantify dystrophin restoration, fibrosis (Masson's trichrome, picrosirius red), and inflammatory markers.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first detailed timeline linking dystrophin restoration to specific metrics of cardiac functional recovery and structural normalization. It will define the kinetics of rescue, identify which functional parameters are most responsive to therapy, and establish critical benchmarks for translating editing strategies to DMD patients with established cardiomyopathy.
proposals.novelty: While prior work demonstrates dystrophin correction, this proposal uniquely integrates longitudinal, high-resolution functional imaging with detailed molecular histopathology across the disease course to map the trajectory of cardiac rescue, moving beyond a simple endpoint assessment.

proposals.title: Single-Cell Transcriptomic Profiling of CRISPR-Edited Muscle Tissues Reveals Cell-Type-Specific Editing Efficacy and Off-Target Transcriptional Heterogeneity
proposals.motivation: CRISPR-based genomic editing holds immense promise for treating muscular dystrophies and vascular smooth muscle disorders, as demonstrated by Dr. Olson's recent work. However, current bulk-tissue analyses mask critical heterogeneity in editing efficiency and potential off-target effects across diverse cell types within complex muscle tissue, including myofibers, satellite cells, fibroblasts, and immune cells. A comprehensive, cell-type-resolved assessment is essential for predicting therapeutic efficacy and safety.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that CRISPR-Cas9 editing efficiency and the resulting transcriptomic consequences in muscle are highly heterogeneous across distinct cell populations within the tissue. Furthermore, we predict that specific cell types, particularly resident stem/progenitor cells, will exhibit unique transcriptional responses to editing that are not apparent in bulk analyses.
proposals.method: We will perform single-nucleus RNA sequencing (snRNA-seq) on skeletal and cardiac muscle from humanized mouse models of Duchenne muscular dystrophy and ACTA2-related smooth muscle dysfunction, comparing CRISPR-edited and control tissues. Cell types will be identified and annotated using established reference atlases and clustering algorithms (Seurat v4). Editing efficiency per cell type will be quantified by aligning sequencing reads to the target locus. Differential expression analysis (MAST, Wilcoxon rank-sum test) will identify cell-type-specific transcriptional changes, including unintended pathway dysregulation.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first high-resolution map of cell-type-specific outcomes of therapeutic gene editing in muscle, directly informing which cellular compartments are successfully corrected. It will establish a robust framework for assessing the transcriptomic safety of editing strategies beyond simple off-target DNA cleavage, identifying subtle, cell-type-specific dysregulations that could impact long-term tissue function.
proposals.novelty: While bulk sequencing and single-cell atlases of muscle exist, the direct integration of single-cell genomics to deconvolve the effects of in vivo CRISPR editing across all resident cell types in disease models is a novel and critical step toward precision gene therapy. This moves beyond confirming genomic correction to understanding its functional consequence in each cellular niche.

proposals.title: Directed Evolution of Muscle-Specific Cas9 Variants for Enhanced Precision in Cardiovascular and Smooth Muscle Gene Editing
proposals.motivation: CRISPR-Cas9 gene editing holds immense therapeutic promise for inherited muscle diseases, including Duchenne muscular dystrophy and smooth muscle disorders like ACTA2-related syndromes. However, a major translational hurdle is the risk of off-target editing in non-muscle tissues, which could lead to adverse effects. Current high-fidelity Cas9 variants improve general specificity but lack cell-type selectivity, limiting their safety and efficacy for in vivo applications targeting muscle.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying directed evolution to the Cas9 protein can generate novel variants with enhanced binding affinity or activity specifically within muscle cell lineages (cardiomyocytes, smooth muscle cells, and skeletal myofibers), thereby achieving higher on-target precision in therapeutically relevant tissues.
proposals.method: We will employ a yeast surface display-based directed evolution platform. A library of SpCas9 mutants, focused on residues involved in DNA interaction and PAM recognition, will be displayed on yeast. Sequential rounds of selection will be performed using biotinylated DNA targets containing muscle-specific epigenetic signatures (e.g., open chromatin regions from ATAC-seq data of human iPSC-derived cardiomyocytes) under competitive pressure with non-muscle DNA targets. Positive clones will be validated in vitro for editing efficiency and specificity in patient-derived muscle cells and in a humanized mouse model of muscular dystrophy.
proposals.contribution: This work will yield a new toolkit of muscle-optimized Cas9 editors, directly applicable to Olson lab's focus on correcting pathogenic variants in muscle genes. It will provide a novel strategy to enhance the tissue-specificity of genomic editing, a critical step towards safer in vivo gene therapies for cardiovascular and musculoskeletal diseases.
proposals.novelty: While directed evolution has been used to alter Cas9 PAM specificity, its application to evolve cell-type-selective activity based on native chromatin landscapes is novel. This approach moves beyond general fidelity to create editors intrinsically biased for therapeutically relevant muscle cell environments.

proposals.title: Acute Adult-Onset Deletion of Rbpms Reveals Its Role as a Non-Compensated Hub in the Post-Developmental Cardiac Gene Regulatory Network
proposals.motivation: RBPMS is an RNA-binding protein essential for heart development, as constitutive knockout is embryonic lethal. However, its function in the mature adult heart is unknown. Determining if RBPMS is a critical, non-redundant hub in the adult cardiac gene regulatory network has direct relevance for understanding the stability of cardiac cellular identity and the potential for adult-onset cardiomyopathies arising from dysregulation of such hubs.
proposals.hypothesis: Acute, cardiomyocyte-specific deletion of Rbpms in adult mice will recapitulate the severe structural and functional deficits seen in constitutive knockouts, demonstrating that RBPMS is a critical network component with no compensatory capacity in the mature heart.
proposals.method: We will generate Rbpms-floxed mice and cross them with mice expressing a tamoxifen-inducible, cardiomyocyte-specific Cre (MerCreMer). Adult (8-12 week) double-transgenic and control littermates will receive tamoxifen to induce Rbpms deletion. Cardiac phenotype will be assessed longitudinally via echocardiography, followed by terminal analysis of hypertrophy, fibrosis, and molecular profiling (RNA-seq) at defined timepoints post-deletion. These outcomes will be directly compared to data from developmental-stage constitutive knockouts.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively separate the developmental role of RBPMS from its homeostatic function, testing the 'keystone hub' model in a post-mitotic tissue. It will establish a paradigm for assessing the compensatory capacity of cardiac regulatory networks and identify RBPMS as a potential vulnerability point for adult cardiac disease.
proposals.novelty: While prior work established RBPMS's developmental role, this study directly tests the untested assumption that its lethality is purely developmental by applying an inducible, adult-specific knockout strategy—a network stability question not previously addressed for this cardiac regulator.

proposals.title: Temporal Requirement of RBPMS in the Adult Heart: Testing the Irreversibility of Post-Developmental Loss
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 studies by the Olson lab and others have established the RNA-binding protein RBPMS as a critical regulator of cardiac development and smooth muscle function, with constitutive knockout leading to severe cardiomyopathy. However, it is unknown whether RBPMS is required for the maintenance of cardiac function in the adult heart or if its essential role is restricted to developmental programming. This distinction is crucial for assessing its therapeutic relevance for adult-onset heart disease.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the severe cardiac phenotype caused by RBPMS loss is developmentally programmed and irreversible, such that acute, adult-onset deletion of Rbpms will not recapitulate the constitutive knockout phenotype.
proposals.method: We will employ tamoxifen-inducible, cardiomyocyte-specific Cre (Myh6-MerCreMer) drivers crossed with Rbpms-floxed mice to achieve acute Rbpms deletion in adult cardiomyocytes. Cardiac function will be serially assessed by echocardiography. We will compare these mice to constitutive, developmental Rbpms knockout models using established histological, molecular (RNA-seq), and functional endpoints. CRISPR-Cas9-mediated editing in induced pluripotent stem cell-derived cardiomyocytes (iPSC-CMs) with a doxycycline-inducible Cas9 system will provide a complementary human cellular model for acute RBPMS loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively determine if RBPMS is a maintenance factor for adult cardiac homeostasis or a developmental determinant with a legacy effect. It will clarify the therapeutic window for targeting RBPMS-related pathways and advance our understanding of how developmental RNA-binding programs constrain adult heart function.
proposals.novelty: While RBPMS's developmental role is known, testing the temporal requirement of its function in the adult myocardium using inducible genetic tools is novel. This directly addresses a fundamental gap in understanding whether the phenotype is due to loss of a developmental instruction or an ongoing maintenance function.

proposals.title: Systematic Identification of RBPMS Splicing Regulatory Motifs Beyond the CAC Core Using High-Throughput iCLIP and Deep Learning
proposals.motivation: The RNA-binding protein RBPMS is a critical regulator of alternative splicing in cardiovascular and smooth muscle development, with its known CAC motif being insufficient to explain its target specificity and functional impact. Pre-2026 studies by the Olson lab and others have shown that manipulating splicing via gene editing can rescue disease phenotypes, but a comprehensive understanding of RBPMS's cis-regulatory code is lacking. Defining its full binding lexicon is essential for rationally designing therapies for muscular dystrophies and vascular smooth muscle disorders.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that RBPMS recognizes a composite, context-dependent regulatory element consisting of the core CAC motif plus adjacent sequence or structural features that dictate its splicing outcomes.
proposals.method: We will perform iCLIP-seq on patient-derived induced vascular smooth muscle cells (iVSMCs) and induced cardiomyocytes (iCMs) to map RBPMS-RNA interactions at nucleotide resolution. Enriched sequences and secondary structure propensities from crosslink sites will be analyzed using the MEME suite and RNAfold. A convolutional neural network (CNN) model will be trained on these high-confidence binding sites to predict novel motif combinations and their association with RBPMS-dependent splicing changes quantified by RNA-seq following CRISPRi-mediated RBPMS knockdown.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first high-resolution map of RBPMS binding determinants in relevant human muscle cell types, moving beyond the canonical motif. It will provide a predictive model linking specific RBPMS-bound motifs to splicing decisions, creating a framework for interpreting variants in non-coding regions of genes like ACTA2 and TTN. This directly informs future precision gene editing strategies to modulate splicing for therapeutic benefit.
proposals.novelty: While the CAC motif is established, a systematic search for auxiliary features governing RBPMS splicing regulation in disease-relevant human muscle cells has not been conducted. Integrating iCLIP with a deep learning approach to decipher a context-dependent 'splicing code' for RBPMS represents a novel, mechanism-driven advance.

proposals.title: Rescue of Cardiac Contractile Dysfunction by Targeted Correction of RBPMS-Dependent Splicing in Non-Sarcomeric Transcripts
proposals.motivation: RNA-binding protein with multiple splicing (RBPMS) is a critical regulator of alternative splicing in cardiomyocytes, with its knockout leading to severe contractile defects. While its direct sarcomeric gene targets are known, the functional contribution of its non-sarcomeric splicing targets to the disease phenotype remains unexplored. Determining if correcting these non-sarcomeric splicing events can rescue function is essential for understanding the hierarchy of disease mediators in cardiomyopathies and for broadening therapeutic targets beyond core contractile proteins.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that RBPMS-dependent splicing dysregulation of non-sarcomeric transcripts—involved in calcium handling, metabolism, and cytoskeletal organization—is a primary driver of the contractile dysfunction observed in RBPMS-deficient cardiomyocytes, and that correcting these specific splicing defects will rescue the phenotype.
proposals.method: We will use CRISPR/Cas9 to generate an RBPMS-knockout in human induced pluripotent stem cell-derived cardiomyocytes (iPSC-CMs). RNA-seq and CLIP-seq will identify direct, dysregulated non-sarcomeric splicing targets. For each top candidate, we will design and deliver CRISPR-Cas13d-based RNA base editors (e.g., REPAIR or RESCUE systems) to correct the specific aberrant splice junction in the mature mRNA without altering genomic DNA. Contractile function will be assessed via video-based sarcomere shortening assays and calcium transient imaging.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a causal link between the splicing of specific non-sarcomeric genes and core contractile performance, moving beyond the sarcomere-centric view of cardiomyopathy. It will validate RNA-targeting CRISPR tools for multiplexed correction of splicing defects in cardiac cells. The findings could identify novel, tractable therapeutic nodes for heart failure that operate upstream of final contractile output.
proposals.novelty: While prior work focuses on correcting mutations in sarcomere genes, this proposal tests the non-obvious premise that correcting splicing in non-sarcomeric RBPMS targets is sufficient to rescue a primary sarcomere function defect. It uniquely applies RNA-editing, rather than genomic DNA editing, to dissect a splicing regulator's disease mechanism.

proposals.title: CRISPR/Cas9-Mediated Genetic Dissection of RBPMS Function in Pathological Cardiac Hypertrophy
proposals.motivation: Pathological cardiac hypertrophy, driven by pressure overload, is a maladaptive response that progresses to heart failure. RNA-binding proteins (RBPs) are critical post-transcriptional regulators of cardiac gene expression, but their roles in stress responses remain underexplored. RBPMS is a conserved RBP implicated in smooth muscle and endothelial cell function, yet its expression dynamics and functional necessity in cardiomyocytes during hemodynamic stress are completely unknown, representing a significant gap in understanding the molecular circuitry of hypertrophy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that RBPMS expression is dynamically regulated in cardiomyocytes in response to pressure overload and that RBPMS functions as a critical RNA-regulatory node, either promoting adaptive gene expression or restraining maladaptive pathways during the development of pathological hypertrophy.
proposals.method: We will employ a multi-tiered genomic editing strategy in established mouse models. First, we will generate a novel RBPMS-2A-tdTomato reporter allele via CRISPR/Cas9 in C57BL/6 zygotes to longitudinally track RBPMS expression by flow cytometry and imaging in cardiomyocytes isolated from mice subjected to transverse aortic constriction (TAC). Second, we will create a cardiomyocyte-specific, inducible RBPMS knockout mouse line (using αMHC-MerCreMer and LoxP-flanked Rbpms alleles) to assess the functional consequence of RBPMS loss during TAC-induced hypertrophy via echocardiography, histology, and RNA-sequencing. Third, we will use AAV9-mediated delivery of CRISPR activation/interference systems to modulate RBPMS levels specifically in adult mouse hearts post-TAC.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first causal link between RBPMS and the cardiac response to hemodynamic stress, defining it as either a protective or detrimental factor. It will generate two novel, publicly available genetically engineered mouse models (reporter and conditional knockout) for the community. The findings could identify RBPMS as a novel RNA-regulatory target for modulating the hypertrophic gene program.
proposals.novelty: While RBPMS has been studied in vascular development, its role in adult cardiomyocyte biology and stress response is entirely unexplored. This proposal uniquely integrates state-of-the-art, inducible genetic editing tools—a hallmark of the Olson lab—to move beyond correlation and establish definitive in vivo function for this RBP in the stressed heart.

proposals.title: Functional Optical Mapping Guides a Disaggregase Screen to Rescue Arrhythmogenic Protein Clusters in RBPMS-Deficient Cardiomyocytes
proposals.motivation: RNA-binding proteins like RBPMS are critical for cardiac splicing, and their deficiency leads to arrhythmogenic perinuclear protein aggregates, such as phospholamban (PLN) clusters. Current anti-arrhythmic drugs treat electrical symptoms, not the underlying proteinopathy. Disaggregase compounds, explored in neurodegenerative diseases, offer a promising but untested therapeutic strategy for cardiac proteinopathies, necessitating a functional assay to link aggregate dissolution directly to electrophysiological rescue.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific perinuclear protein aggregates in RBPMS-deficient cardiomyocytes are directly responsible for arrhythmic phenotypes, and that candidate disaggregase compounds can simultaneously dissolve these aggregates and normalize cardiac electrophysiology.
