proposals.method: We will use CRISPR-Cas9 to generate RBPMS-knockout human induced pluripotent stem cell-derived cardiomyocytes (iPSC-CMs). High-content imaging will quantify perinuclear PLN aggregates. Optical mapping with voltage- and calcium-sensitive dyes will measure conduction velocity (CV), action potential duration (APD), and arrhythmia inducibility in engineered heart tissues. A library of ~500 candidate disaggregase compounds (e.g., from neuroprotective screens) will be applied, and optical mapping will serve as the primary functional readout to identify hits that normalize CV and APD while reducing aggregates, validated by confocal imaging and RNA-seq of corrected splicing events.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, function-first pipeline for discovering anti-arrhythmic therapies that target causative protein aggregates. It will directly demonstrate the therapeutic potential of disaggregases for cardiac disease and provide a mechanistic link between RBPMS-regulated splicing, protein clustering, and arrhythmogenesis.
proposals.novelty: The innovation lies in using real-time optical mapping of cardiac electrophysiology as the primary screening tool to guide disaggregase discovery, moving beyond correlative imaging to establish a direct functional link between molecular intervention and tissue-level rescue, a strategy not previously applied in cardiology.

proposals.title: Direct Pharmacological Modulation of RBPMS via CETSA-MS Identifies Chemical Probes for Smooth Muscle Cell Reprogramming
proposals.motivation: The RNA-binding protein RBPMS is a critical regulator of smooth muscle cell (SMC) phenotype and plasticity, with established roles in cardiovascular development and disease. Despite its importance, no direct pharmacological modulators of RBPMS exist, limiting our ability to probe its function therapeutically. This gap is significant given the Olson lab's expertise in using genomic editing to correct SMC dysfunction, as small-molecule modulators could offer a complementary, reversible strategy for phenotypic modulation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an unbiased Cellular Thermal Shift Assay coupled with Mass Spectrometry (CETSA-MS) screen in live cells will identify small molecules that directly bind and stabilize RBPMS, and that these compounds will functionally modulate RBPMS-dependent alternative splicing and mRNA stability in native SMC contexts.
proposals.method: We will implement a CETSA-MS platform using induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived SMCs or primary aortic SMCs expressing endogenous RBPMS. Cells will be treated with a diverse small-molecule library, subjected to heat denaturation, and RBPMS stabilization will be quantified via quantitative mass spectrometry. Primary hits will be validated in secondary CETSA and SPR assays for direct binding. Functional validation will employ RNA-seq and CLIP-seq in treated cells to assess changes in RBPMS-dependent alternative splicing (e.g., of MYLK, ACTA2) and mRNA stability of known targets, moving beyond artificial reporter systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first set of high-confidence chemical probes that directly engage RBPMS, providing essential tools for dissecting its role in SMC biology. It will establish a CETSA-MS pipeline applicable to other RNA-binding proteins central to cardiovascular and muscular diseases. The project bridges the gap between genomic editing strategies and pharmacological intervention for modulating cell fate.
proposals.novelty: While CETSA-MS is established for soluble enzymes, its application to identify direct binders of a context-dependent RNA-binding protein like RBPMS in live, physiologically relevant cells is novel. This approach moves beyond indirect, reporter-based screening to directly link target engagement with native molecular function.

proposals.title: A Dual-Reporter Splicing Screen Identifies Small Molecule Enhancers of RBPMS Activity for Cardiovascular and Smooth Muscle Reprogramming
proposals.motivation: The RNA-binding protein RBPMS is a critical regulator of alternative splicing in cardiovascular development and smooth muscle cell plasticity, with direct relevance to heart disease and vascular pathologies. However, the lack of pharmacological tools to modulate its activity limits therapeutic exploration and functional studies. High-throughput methods to identify RBPMS modulators are needed to bridge this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: A mammalian dual-fluorescent reporter system, adapted from protein engineering selection strategies, can robustly and specifically identify small molecules that enhance RBPMS-dependent splicing activity in a high-throughput screen.
proposals.method: We will engineer a stably integrated reporter construct in C2C12 myoblasts or induced pluripotent stem cell-derived smooth muscle cells. The construct contains a constitutive TagBFP reporter and a splicing-sensitive GFP reporter where GFP expression depends on RBPMS-mediated inclusion of a critical exon. Using CRISPR-Cas9 editing, we will create an isogenic RBPMS-knockout line as a negative control. We will screen the NIH Clinical Collection and Mechanistic Diversity Set libraries via automated fluorescence microscopy, using the BFP:GFP ratio to normalize for cell health and identify hits that specifically increase splicing-dependent GFP signal.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first pharmacological enhancers of RBPMS activity, providing novel chemical probes to study its role in muscle biology. It will establish a generalizable screening platform for RNA-binding protein modulators, directly advancing the goal of manipulating splicing for cardiovascular therapy.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in adapting a dual-reporter genetic selection paradigm from directed enzyme evolution to the mammalian cell-based discovery of splicing modulators, enabling normalized, high-fidelity detection of RBPMS activity changes in a compound screen.

proposals.title: A High-Throughput Splicing Reporter Screen Identifies Small Molecule Modulators of RBPMS Activity for Cardiovascular and Smooth Muscle Applications
proposals.motivation: RNA-binding protein with multiple splicing (RBPMS) is a critical regulator of alternative splicing in cardiovascular and smooth muscle development and function. Pre-2026 studies indicate its role in promoting smooth muscle cell contractile phenotypes and modulating cardiac gene expression, but its therapeutic potential is untapped due to a lack of pharmacological tools. Identifying small molecules that enhance RBPMS activity could provide a novel, non-genomic strategy to correct pathogenic splicing defects in diseases like multisystemic smooth muscle dysfunction syndrome or heart failure.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a high-throughput, cell-based splicing reporter assay can identify small molecules that enhance RBPMS-mediated alternative splicing, and that these compounds will promote a functional, contractile phenotype in patient-derived smooth muscle cells with splicing-related pathologies.
proposals.method: We will engineer a stable HEK293T cell line harboring a luciferase-based, RBPMS-responsive splicing reporter construct, where luciferase activity is dependent on RBPMS-induced exon inclusion. This cell line will be used to screen the ~200,000-compound NIH Molecular Libraries Small Molecule Repository in a 384-well plate format. Primary hits will be validated in secondary assays using RT-PCR to measure endogenous RBPMS-dependent splicing events in induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived smooth muscle cells. Lead compounds will be tested for their ability to rescue the contractile deficit in smooth muscle cells derived from patients with ACTA2 pathogenic variants.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first known pharmacological modulators of RBPMS, establishing a new chemical toolset for studying RNA biology in muscle. It will validate RBPMS as a druggable target for correcting splicing defects, offering a potential therapeutic avenue distinct from direct genomic editing. The project will also provide a generalizable screening platform for identifying modulators of other splicing regulators relevant to cardiovascular disease.
proposals.novelty: While Dr. Olson's lab has pioneered genomic editing to correct specific pathogenic variants, this proposal shifts the paradigm to target a master regulatory RNA-binding protein, offering a potential single therapeutic for multiple mutations affecting the same splicing network. The development of a functional, high-throughput splicing reporter for RBPMS is itself a novel reagent not described in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Direct Nanomechanical Profiling of Single Sarcomeres Reveals RBPMS as a Regulator of Cardiac Passive Mechanics
proposals.motivation: The RNA-binding protein RBPMS is implicated in cardiac development and function, but its specific role in regulating the fundamental contractile unit, the sarcomere, remains unknown. Pre-2026 studies using gene editing in models of muscular dystrophy and smooth muscle disease demonstrate the power of linking genetic perturbation to mechanical function, yet current assays for cardiomyocyte mechanics (e.g., traction force microscopy, whole-cell AFM) lack the resolution to isolate the contribution of a single sarcomere. This gap hinders the identification of precise molecular mechanisms underlying genetic cardiomyopathies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that RBPMS knockout alters the intrinsic passive mechanical properties of the cardiac sarcomere, specifically increasing its resting tension and stiffness, which contributes to diastolic dysfunction at the cellular level.
proposals.method: We will adapt single-molecule force spectroscopy to the sarcomere scale. Induced pluripotent stem cell-derived cardiomyocytes (iPSC-CMs) from isogenic RBPMS knockout and control lines will be permeabilized and adhered to a substrate. An AFM tip will be functionalized with antibodies against α-actinin-2 to specifically tether to a single Z-disc. Using a force-ramp protocol, we will perform repeated extension-retraction cycles on the targeted sarcomere to measure force-extension curves. These curves will be analyzed with a worm-like chain model modified for sarcomeric proteins to extract quantitative parameters of passive tension, stiffness, and viscoelastic hysteresis.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a first-of-its-kind method for direct, specific mechanical interrogation of a single sarcomere. It will definitively determine whether RBPMS regulates cardiac mechanics at the most fundamental structural level, providing a novel mechanistic link between this RNA-binding protein and sarcomere integrity. The platform will be broadly applicable for probing the nanomechanical effects of other cardiomyopathy-associated gene variants.
proposals.novelty: While AFM is used on cardiomyocytes, existing studies measure global cell stiffness; the novelty lies in the specific antibody-based tethering strategy to mechanically isolate a single, defined sarcomere, analogous to single-molecule studies but scaled to a functional macromolecular complex.

proposals.title: Direct Nanomechanical Disruption of Sarcomere Function by Phospholamban Aggregates in Cardiomyopathy
proposals.motivation: Mutations in phospholamban (PLN), such as R14del, cause severe dilated cardiomyopathy, but the precise pathogenic mechanism remains unclear. While PLN is known to regulate SERCA2a and calcium cycling, emerging evidence suggests mutant PLN forms cytotoxic aggregates, yet it is unknown if these aggregates directly and physically disrupt the fundamental contractile unit, the sarcomere, independent of calcium defects. Establishing this direct physical link is critical for validating aggregation as a primary therapeutic target for gene editing or anti-aggregation therapies.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that mutant PLN aggregates directly impair sarcomere structure and force generation by physically disrupting Z-disc or thin filament integrity, and that this nanomechanical dysfunction is a primary driver of cardiomyopathy, occurring prior to or independent of measurable calcium handling defects.
proposals.method: We will employ high-resolution atomic force microscopy (AFM) in force spectroscopy and peak force tapping modes on isolated adult mouse and patient-derived induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-cardiomyocytes. We will generate isogenic iPSC lines using CRISPR-Cas9 to introduce the PLN R14del mutation and knockout RBPMS, a factor linked to aggregate formation. AFM will directly measure local stiffness, adhesion, and intra-sarcomere ultrastructure at Z-discs and I-bands, correlating with simultaneous measurements of single-sarcomere contractile force using AFM-based nanomechanical mapping.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct nanomechanical evidence linking a specific protein aggregate to sarcomere dysfunction, moving beyond correlative biochemical studies. It will establish a foundational biomechanical paradigm for PLN-related and potentially other protein aggregation cardiomyopathies, directly informing the development of targeted genomic editing strategies, like those Dr. Olson's lab pioneers, aimed at preventing aggregate formation.
proposals.novelty: While others have studied PLN aggregation biochemically and calcium handling electrophysiologically, no study has directly probed the physical impact of aggregates on sarcomere nanomechanics. Fusing single-sarcomere biomechanics with genetically engineered aggregation models represents a novel, causal approach to cardiomyopathy mechanism.

proposals.title: Context-Dependent Synthetic Lethality Screens Identify Core and Compensatory RNA-Binding Protein Networks in Muscle and Smooth Muscle Lineages
proposals.motivation: RNA-binding proteins (RBPs) like RBPMS are critical post-transcriptional regulators of cell identity and function, and their dysfunction is implicated in cardiovascular and muscular diseases. However, functional redundancy and compensatory mechanisms within RBP networks pose a major challenge for therapeutic targeting, as inhibiting one RBP may be buffered by others in a context-specific manner. A systematic, comparative mapping of these compensatory interactions across relevant cellular states is lacking but essential for identifying robust therapeutic nodes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that genetic depletion of RBPMS will reveal a landscape of synthetic lethal interactions with other RBPs, and that the composition and topology of this compensatory network will vary significantly between cellular contexts, including differentiated muscle, smooth muscle, and their progenitor states.
proposals.method: We will engineer isogenic induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC) lines with a doxycycline-inducible CRISPR-Cas9 system for RBPMS knockout. These iPSCs will be differentiated into cardiomyocytes, skeletal myotubes, and vascular smooth muscle cells to establish distinct contexts. In parallel, we will perform barcoded, pooled CRISPRi/a screens targeting a library of ~1,500 human RBPs in each context upon RBPMS depletion. Genetic interaction scores will be calculated using the MAGeCK-VISPR algorithm. Network analysis will employ hierarchical clustering and community detection algorithms (e.g., Louvain method) to identify core versus context-specific synthetic lethal modules.
proposals.contribution: This work will generate the first comparative map of RBP compensatory networks in muscle lineages, directly identifying candidate RBPs whose inhibition is synthetically lethal with RBPMS loss. It will provide a framework for predicting which therapeutic targets may have broad efficacy versus context-specific utility. The validated hits will offer new, potentially druggable pathways for conditions involving RBPMS dysfunction.
proposals.novelty: While synthetic lethal screens are established, applying them in parallel across multiple, precisely engineered isogenic cellular contexts to deconvolute RBP network plasticity is a novel strategy. This moves beyond identifying interactions in a single cell type to mapping a 'contextual interactome,' which is critical for translating basic RBP biology into targeted therapies.

proposals.title: Targeting Phospholamban Aggregation as a Primary Therapeutic Strategy for R14del Cardiomyopathy
proposals.motivation: The R14del mutation in phospholamban (PLN) causes a severe, often fatal dilated cardiomyopathy with arrhythmias. The dominant disease model posits that mutant PLN disrupts calcium handling in the sarcoplasmic reticulum as the primary pathogenic event. However, this model does not fully explain the aggressive, proteinopathy-like progression observed in patients and animal models, suggesting a critical knowledge gap in disease etiology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the R14del mutation primarily drives cardiomyopathy by inducing toxic PLN protein aggregation, and that calcium handling defects are a secondary consequence of this proteostatic collapse.
proposals.method: We will use CRISPR/Cas9 to correct the R14del mutation in patient-derived induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) and differentiate them into cardiomyocytes (iPSC-CMs). In parallel, we will generate isogenic R14del iPSC-CMs. We will employ super-resolution microscopy and biochemical fractionation to quantify and characterize endogenous PLN aggregates. Calcium transients and contractility will be measured in parallel. Using AAV9 vectors, we will deliver aggregation-inhibiting peptides (e.g., based on β-sheet breaker designs) and CRISPR-mediated transcriptional repression of the mutant allele into a pre-existing humanized PLN-R14del mouse model, assessing rescue of cardiac function, histology, and aggregate burden.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively test the causal hierarchy between aggregation and calcium dysregulation in PLN cardiomyopathy. It will establish a novel disease mechanism centered on proteotoxicity and validate a new therapeutic paradigm focused on inhibiting aggregation or clearing mutant protein, moving beyond solely correcting calcium kinetics.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the established pathogenic sequence for PLN-R14del, challenging the field's calcium-centric view. It uniquely combines patient-specific iPSC-CMs, a humanized mouse model, and targeted anti-aggregation interventions—methods proven in Olson lab studies on ACTA2 and DMD—to test aggregation as the primary driver.

proposals.title: Causal role of DWORF suppression in the initiation of dilated cardiomyopathy: A gene editing and cellular reprogramming study
proposals.motivation: DWORF (Dwarf Open Reading Frame) is a micropeptide that enhances SERCA2a activity by displacing its inhibitors, phospholamban and sarcolipin, and its expression is reduced in human and murine heart failure. However, it is unknown whether this reduction is a cause or a consequence of pathological remodeling. Determining causality is critical for evaluating DWORF as a novel therapeutic target for dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM).
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that targeted suppression of DWORF expression in healthy cardiomyocytes is sufficient to initiate a pathological cascade leading to DCM, establishing it as a primary driver of disease rather than a secondary effect.
proposals.method: We will use CRISPR interference (CRISPRi) with a catalytically dead Cas9 (dCas9) fused to the KRAB repressor domain to specifically and stably suppress endogenous DWORF expression in human induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived cardiomyocytes (iPSC-CMs). In parallel, we will generate a novel conditional, cardiomyocyte-specific DWORF knockout mouse model using Cre-loxP technology. Functional consequences will be assessed in iPSC-CMs via calcium transient imaging and contractility assays, and in mice via serial echocardiography and invasive hemodynamics, prior to the onset of overt heart failure.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively establish the causal role of DWORF downregulation in initiating DCM, shifting the paradigm of its function in disease. It will validate DWORF as a high-priority target for gene therapy or pharmacological activation strategies aimed at preventing heart failure progression.
proposals.novelty: While prior work correlates DWORF loss with heart failure, this study directly tests causality by inducing suppression in a pre-pathological state. It uniquely combines precise genomic editing in human iPSC-CMs with a novel inducible mouse model to dissect the initiating events of cardiomyopathy.

proposals.title: A CRISPRi and Proximity Proteomics Screen Identifies the SR Proteostasis Network Mediating DWORF-Dependent Clearance of PLN-R14del Aggregates
proposals.motivation: The PLN-R14del mutation causes dilated cardiomyopathy via toxic perinuclear aggregates of phospholamban (PLN) that disrupt sarcoplasmic reticulum (SR) function. The micropeptide DWORF has been shown to reduce these aggregates, offering a promising therapeutic avenue, but its mechanism is unknown. A systems-level understanding of how DWORF engages the SR proteostasis network is critical for developing targeted interventions for this fatal disease.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that DWORF clears PLN-R14del aggregates by orchestrating a specific, multi-component proteostasis network at the SR membrane, involving both direct protein-protein interactions and the regulation of broader degradation pathways.
proposals.method: We will perform an unbiased genome-wide CRISPR interference (CRISPRi) screen in isogenic PLN-R14del cardiomyocytes to identify genetic modifiers of DWORF's aggregate-clearing function. In parallel, we will use BioID2 proximity labeling in these cells to map DWORF's direct interactome at the SR. Comparative SR proteomics (via differential centrifugation and LC-MS/MS) will define the proteomic landscape changes induced by DWORF expression. Top hits from these orthogonal datasets will be functionally validated in patient-derived PLN-R14del iPSC-cardiomyocytes using CRISPR/Cas9 knockout and high-content imaging of aggregates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive map of the SR proteostasis network activated by DWORF, moving beyond a single target to a network model. It will identify novel, druggable components essential for clearing pathogenic protein aggregates in cardiomyopathy, directly informing future combination therapies.
proposals.novelty: While prior work established DWORF's phenotypic effect, this study uniquely applies systematic, unbiased screening and proteomics to deconstruct its mechanism within the native SR environment of diseased human cardiomyocytes, revealing the network logic rather than a single linear pathway.

proposals.title: Titratable DWORF Gene Therapy to Define a Therapeutic Window in a Mouse Model of Heterozygous PLN-R14del Cardiomyopathy
proposals.motivation: The heterozygous PLN-R14del mutation is a common cause of dilated cardiomyopathy, where mutant phospholamban (PLN) dominantly inhibits SERCA2a, impairing calcium handling. While the micropeptide DWORF can displace PLN from SERCA2a, its therapeutic application in heterozygous patients is complicated by the need to counteract the mutant without excessively inhibiting essential wild-type PLN function. This mirrors the challenge in oncology of targeting heterozygous driver mutations without ablating the wild-type protein's vital function, necessitating a defined therapeutic window.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a precise, intermediate level of DWORF overexpression will create a therapeutic window, optimally restoring SERCA2a function and cardiac performance in heterozygous PLN-R14del mice by counteracting the mutant without causing dysfunction from excessive wild-type PLN inhibition.
proposals.method: We will generate a titratable AAV9 vector system (e.g., using a doxycycline-inducible or liver-specific promoter) to deliver DWORF to adult heterozygous PLN-R14del mice. Cohorts will receive different vector doses or inducer concentrations to establish a gradient of cardiac DWORF expression. Cardiac function will be assessed longitudinally by echocardiography and invasive hemodynamics, with terminal analyses of calcium transients, SERCA2a activity, and molecular profiling of hypertrophy and stress pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the dose-response relationship for DWORF in a clinically relevant heterozygous model, identifying the optimal therapeutic window for SERCA2a potentiation. It will provide a critical preclinical framework for a safe, titratable gene therapy strategy for PLN-R14del cardiomyopathy and a paradigm for treating other dominant-negative disorders.
proposals.novelty: This study innovatively adapts the oncology concept of a 'therapeutic window' for a heterozygous genetic heart disease, moving beyond simple gene overexpression to a precision dosing strategy tailored to the patient's genotype.

proposals.title: A Longitudinal Cohort Study of Cardiac Aging: Lifespan and Functional Trajectories in a Murine Model of Sustained DWORF Overexpression
proposals.motivation: The sarcoplasmic reticulum micropeptide DWORF is a potent activator of the SERCA2a pump, enhancing cardiac contractility and showing therapeutic promise in models of heart failure. However, its long-term, systemic effects on cardiac aging, lifespan, and age-related morbidity remain completely unknown. Determining whether sustained DWORF overexpression confers a durable protective advantage or leads to unforeseen late-life sequelae is critical for evaluating its translational potential as a chronic therapy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that lifelong, cardiac-specific overexpression of DWORF will extend murine lifespan and delay the onset of age-related cardiac dysfunction by maintaining SERCA2a activity and calcium handling efficiency throughout the aging process.
proposals.method: We will establish a defined, genetically engineered cohort of mice with inducible, cardiomyocyte-specific DWORF overexpression (n=80) and littermate controls (n=80). Using a pre-planned longitudinal protocol, animals will undergo serial, non-invasive assessments at 6-month intervals until natural death, including echocardiography for systolic/diastolic function, plasma biomarker profiling (e.g., NT-proBNP, troponin), and standardized frailty scoring. Lifespan will be the primary outcome, analyzed using Kaplan-Meier survival curves and Cox proportional hazards models. Serial functional data will be modeled using linear mixed-effects regression to define aging trajectories.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide the first comprehensive dataset on the lifelong consequences of DWORF overexpression, defining its role in cardiac aging and longevity. It will establish whether DWORF is merely a short-term inotrope or a genuine modifier of the cardiac aging process, directly informing the feasibility of long-term DWORF-based therapies for age-related cardiac decline.
proposals.novelty: This work innovatively adapts the longitudinal cohort study design from human epidemiology to a controlled preclinical setting, enabling the rigorous dissection of lifespan effects and aging trajectories for a genetic intervention, a approach rarely applied to micropeptide studies in cardiology.

proposals.title: A High-Throughput, Function-Coupled Screen for Disaggregases Targeting Pathogenic Phospholamban Aggregates in Human Cardiomyocytes
proposals.motivation: Mutations in phospholamban (PLN), such as R14del, cause dilated cardiomyopathy via dominant-negative protein aggregation and impaired SERCA2a regulation, leading to fatal heart failure. While disaggregase therapies are sought, existing screens identify compounds that clear aggregates but often disrupt the essential physiological role of PLN in regulating calcium cycling, causing cardiotoxicity. A screening strategy that directly couples aggregate dissolution with functional validation in cardiomyocytes is urgently needed to identify truly therapeutic candidates.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a high-content phenotypic screen for perinuclear PLN cluster dissolution, directly coupled in-line with assays of calcium transients and contractility, will identify chemical disaggregases that clear pathogenic aggregates while preserving or restoring normal SERCA2a pump regulation and cardiomyocyte function.
proposals.method: We will use CRISPR/Cas9 to introduce the pathogenic PLN-R14del mutation into human induced pluripotent stem cells (iPSCs) and differentiate them into cardiomyocytes (iPSC-CMs) to model the disease. A high-throughput, automated imaging platform will first screen chemical libraries for compounds that dissolve perinuclear GFP-tagged PLN aggregates. Hits from this primary screen will be automatically subjected, in the same well, to secondary functional assays using the calcium indicator Fluo-4 AM to measure calcium transient kinetics and video-based analysis to quantify contractility. Only compounds that both clear aggregates and normalize calcium handling/contractility without inducing arrhythmia will be advanced.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, integrated screening pipeline that directly links target engagement (aggregate clearance) to physiological outcome in a human cardiomyocyte model. It will yield the first candidate compounds with validated efficacy and safety profiles for treating PLN cardiomyopathy. The platform will serve as a blueprint for developing therapies for other protein aggregation cardiomyopathies.
proposals.novelty: The critical novelty is the mandatory, in-line functional validation within the screening cascade, moving beyond pure phenotypic aggregation readouts. This ensures hits are not just disaggregases but 'function-preserving disaggregases,' a concept not implemented in prior screens for cardiac proteinopathies.

proposals.title: Identification of Compensatory Cofactors for SP2 in NFYa-Mediated Smooth Muscle Cell Reprogramming Using CRISPR-Cas9 Screens
proposals.motivation: The transcription factor complex NFYa is a master regulator of smooth muscle cell (SMC) differentiation and function, and its essential cofactor SP2 is a potential therapeutic target for vascular pathologies. However, genetic compensation or redundancy mechanisms could limit the efficacy of targeting SP2 alone. Identifying alternative cofactors is crucial for understanding the robustness of SMC gene networks and for developing robust gene-editing or reprogramming strategies for cardiovascular disease, such as those seen in ACTA2-related smooth muscle dysfunction syndromes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that one or more transcription factors or chromatin regulators can functionally compensate for the loss of SP2 in maintaining NFYa-driven transcriptional programs in SMCs.
proposals.method: We will perform a genome-wide CRISPR-Cas9 knockout screen in human induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived SMCs with a fluorescent reporter for a key NFYa target gene (e.g., ACTA2 or MYH11). SP2 will be knocked out first to establish a baseline. Using the Brunello genome-wide sgRNA library, we will identify genes whose knockout specifically abrogates reporter expression in the SP2-null background, indicating loss of compensatory activity. Hits will be validated by individual sgRNA-mediated knockout and rescue, followed by assays of SMC contractility, marker expression, and chromatin immunoprecipitation sequencing (ChIP-seq) to assess NFYa binding and histone modifications.
proposals.contribution: This work will map the genetic interactors of the NFYa/SP2 axis, revealing the backup circuitry that maintains SMC identity. It will provide a ranked list of novel cofactors with potential as secondary therapeutic targets or modifiers for gene-editing approaches aimed at SMC pathologies. The findings will advance the field of cardiovascular cell reprogramming by defining core and compensatory components of the SMC transcriptional machinery.
proposals.novelty: While NFYa's role is established, a systematic search for compensatory cofactors in SMCs using a synthetic lethal CRISPR screen in a disease-relevant SP2-null context has not been performed. This moves beyond characterizing primary regulators to defining fail-safe mechanisms, a critical step for durable therapeutic intervention.

proposals.title: Genomic and Functional Characterization of NFYa-SP2 Co-Regulated Gene Networks Linking Metabolic and Proliferative Programs in Smooth Muscle Cells
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 studies by the Olson lab and others have established that transcription factors NFYa and SP2 are critical for smooth muscle cell (SMC) function and can be leveraged for cellular reprogramming in cardiovascular contexts. However, the precise genomic targets and functional gene networks co-regulated by these two factors remain undefined. Defining this shared transcriptional program is essential to understand the molecular link between cellular metabolism and proliferation, a nexus central to SMC dysfunction in vascular diseases and regenerative medicine.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that NFYa and SP2 co-occupy and co-activate a specific set of target genes that integrate metabolic and cell-cycle regulatory pathways, and that disruption of this core gene network impairs SMC phenotypic modulation.
proposals.method: We will use CRISPR/Cas9-mediated knock-in of epitope tags (e.g., FLAG-HA) at endogenous NFYa and SP2 loci in human induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived SMCs to perform sequential chromatin immunoprecipitation (Re-ChIP) for identifying genomic loci co-occupied by both factors. Co-bound regions will be linked to target genes via Hi-C chromatin conformation capture data. Transcriptional output will be assessed by RNA-seq following siRNA-mediated dual knockdown. Candidate target genes will be validated by CRISPRa/i-mediated perturbation and functional assays measuring proliferation, metabolic flux (Seahorse), and contractility.
proposals.contribution: This work will define the first comprehensive map of genes directly co-regulated by NFYa and SP2 in SMCs, establishing a mechanistic link between specific metabolic enzymes and proliferative controls. It will provide a novel set of candidate targets for therapeutic modulation of SMC phenotype in conditions like atherosclerosis, hypertension, and for improving cell-based therapies for muscular dystrophy.
proposals.novelty: While NFYa and SP2 have been studied individually, their functional cooperation and shared genomic targets are unknown. This proposal moves beyond single-factor analysis to define a synergistic transcriptional module, integrating cutting-edge genomic editing and multi-omics approaches pre-2026 to dissect a core regulatory circuit.

proposals.title: Spatiotemporal Mapping of Metabolic State Dynamics During Cardiomyocyte Maturation and Lineage Specification In Vivo
proposals.motivation: The metabolic transition from glycolysis to oxidative phosphorylation is a hallmark of cardiomyocyte maturation, essential for building a functional adult heart. Pre-2026, the dynamics and spatial heterogeneity of this metabolic shift during embryonic development remain poorly understood, limiting our ability to correct metabolic deficiencies in congenital heart disease or in vitro-derived cardiomyocytes. This gap is critical given the Olson lab's focus on gene editing and reprogramming for cardiovascular repair, which requires a mature, metabolically competent phenotype.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that distinct metabolic states, defined by ATP/NADH ratios, are spatially and temporally segregated within the developing mouse heart, correlating with specific lineage decisions (e.g., chamber-specific cardiomyocytes, conduction system cells) and functional maturation.
proposals.method: We will generate transgenic mouse embryos expressing established FRET-based biosensors for ATP (ATeam) and NADH (SoNar) under the control of a cardiomyocyte-specific, tamoxifen-inducible MerCreMer system crossed with αMHC-Cre mice. High-resolution live imaging of explained embryonic hearts (E9.5-E18.5) will be performed using multiphoton microscopy to capture FRET signals. Spatial maps of ATP/NADH ratios will be correlated with lineage markers (e.g., HCN4 for pacemaker cells, MLC2v for ventricular cells) via subsequent immunofluorescence. Computational image analysis will use established algorithms (e.g., Fiji/ImageJ with FRET analyzer plugins) to quantify biosensor signals and generate 3D metabolic maps.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first in vivo, spatially resolved atlas of metabolic state during heart development. It will directly link metabolic microenvironments to cell fate decisions, providing a foundational benchmark for assessing the metabolic maturity of gene-edited or reprogrammed cardiomyocytes intended for therapeutic use. The findings will inform strategies to metabolically 'prime' cells for improved engraftment and function.
proposals.novelty: While biosensors are used in cultured cells, their application for longitudinal, high-resolution metabolic imaging in the intact, developing mammalian embryo is novel. Combining this with the lab's expertise in cardiac gene editing creates a unique approach to dissect the metabolic underpinnings of development, beyond static genomic or transcriptomic snapshots.

proposals.title: CRISPR-Cas9 Editing of NFYa in Human iPSC-Derived Cardiac Organoids to Model and Rescue Contractile Dysfunction
proposals.motivation: The transcription factor NFYa is a critical regulator of smooth muscle cell (SMC) gene programs, and its dysfunction is implicated in vascular pathologies. However, its role in human cardiomyocyte maturation, contractility, and disease within a complex 3D tissue context remains poorly defined. Pre-2026, Olson's lab has pioneered gene editing to rescue monogenic cardiovascular disorders in mice, but translating these findings requires scalable, human-relevant models that capture multicellular interactions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that targeted genomic disruption of NFYa in human induced pluripotent stem cell (iPSC)-derived cardiac organoids will impair cardiomyocyte maturation and contractile function, and that precise correction of NFYa variants will restore normal tissue-level physiology.
proposals.method: We will generate isogenic iPSC lines using CRISPR-Cas9 to create NFYA knockout and patient-specific pathogenic variant lines, alongside corrected controls. These lines will be differentiated into self-assembling 3D cardiac organoids containing cardiomyocytes, fibroblasts, and endothelial cells. Organoid function will be assessed via high-content video microscopy for contractility analysis (using MUSCLEMOTION or similar software), calcium imaging, and single-cell RNA-sequencing (10x Genomics) to define cell-type-specific transcriptional consequences. Structural integrity will be evaluated via immunofluorescence and electron microscopy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel human organoid model for studying NFYa in cardiac development and disease, moving beyond murine models and 2D cultures. It will directly test the translational potential of NFYa-targeted genomic editing in a human tissue context, providing critical preclinical data for future therapeutic strategies for cardiomyopathies and smooth muscle-related disorders.
proposals.novelty: While NFYa has been studied in mouse SMCs, its systematic analysis in a human 3D cardiac organoid system, combined with precise isogenic editing, is unprecedented. This approach integrates Olson's expertise in muscle gene editing with advanced human organoid technology to bridge a key gap between mouse models and clinical translation.

proposals.title: CRISPR/Cas9-Mediated NFYa Reactivation as a Novel Therapeutic Strategy to Reverse Pathological Cardiac Hypertrophy
proposals.motivation: Pathological cardiac hypertrophy, a maladaptive response to pressure overload, is a major precursor to heart failure, a leading cause of mortality with limited curative therapies. Pre-2026 studies identified NFYa as a critical transcriptional regulator of fetal cardiac gene programs, which are silenced postnatally but aberrantly reactivated in disease. Understanding the specific role of NFYa reactivation in adult pathological hypertrophy, distinct from developmental growth, could reveal a new therapeutic node.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that NFYa is reactivated in adult cardiomyocytes during pressure overload-induced pathological hypertrophy and that targeted genomic editing to disrupt this reactivation will prevent or reverse maladaptive cardiac remodeling and dysfunction.
proposals.method: We will use CRISPR/Cas9-based transcriptional interference (CRISPRi) with a catalytically dead Cas9 (dCas9) fused to the KRAB repressor domain to specifically silence the NFYa gene (NFYA) in adult cardiomyocytes in vivo. AAV9 vectors will deliver the dCas9-KRAB and NFYA-targeting sgRNAs to mouse hearts subjected to transverse aortic constriction (TAC). Cardiac function, hypertrophy, fibrosis, and fetal gene re-expression will be assessed by echocardiography, histology, and RNA-seq. Parallel studies will be conducted in human induced pluripotent stem cell-derived cardiomyocytes (iPSC-CMs) subjected to hypertrophic stimuli.
proposals.contribution: This work will definitively establish the causal role of NFYa reactivation in pathological hypertrophy, moving beyond correlation. It will pioneer the use of in vivo CRISPRi in adult cardiomyocytes as a novel gene-editing-based therapeutic modality for heart disease, providing proof-of-concept for targeting transcriptional reprogramming.
proposals.novelty: While NFYa's role in development is known, its function as a reactivated driver of adult disease is unexplored. Applying CRISPRi for targeted gene silencing, rather than knockout, in adult myocardium to modulate a transcriptional regulator represents a novel and potentially safer therapeutic approach compared to permanent genome editing.

ideas.title: Irreversible Network Effects: Testing if RBPMS is a Critical Hub with No Adult Compensatory Capacity
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that the severe phenotype of constitutive RBPMS knockout is solely due to its role in cardiac development. By drawing an analogy from ecology and network science—specifically the concept of a keystone species whose removal causes irreversible collapse in a mature ecosystem—we propose a direct test. The adapted research idea is to employ an inducible, cardiomyocyte-specific Cre system to achieve acute, adult-onset deletion of Rbpms in mice. We then rigorously compare the phenotypic consequences (e.g., hypertrophy, fibrosis, contractile function) to those of the constitutive knockout. This will determine if RBPMS functions as a critical, non-redundant hub in the post-developmental cardiac gene regulatory network, where its loss is irreparable, or if the adult heart possesses compensatory mechanisms absent during development.

ideas.title: Screen for small molecules modulating RBPMS activity via a dual-reporter assay adapted from protein engineering
ideas.description: This expanded idea applies an analogical solution from the field of Directed Evolution and Protein Engineering. In that field, dual-reporter genetic selection systems are used to evolve enzymes with improved function, where one reporter ensures baseline expression and a second reporter is tied to the desired enzymatic activity. We adapt this by developing a mammalian cell-based high-throughput splicing reporter assay with two key outputs: 1) a constitutively expressed fluorescent protein (e.g., TagBFP) to normalize for cell number, compound toxicity, and transfection efficiency, and 2) a splicing-sensitive fluorescent reporter (e.g., GFP) whose correct splicing and expression is directly dependent on RBPMS function. By screening a library of small molecules and analyzing the ratio of splicing-dependent fluorescence to constitutive fluorescence, we can specifically identify drugs that enhance RBPMS activity, scaling the search for therapeutic modulators of this RNA-binding protein.

ideas.description: This study aims to define the comprehensive proteostatic mechanism by which DWORF reduces perinuclear PLN-R14del aggregates, moving beyond a single linear pathway. An initial unbiased CRISPRi screen will identify genetic modifiers of DWORF's aggregate-clearing function, while BioID and comparative SR proteomics will delineate its direct interactors and the broader proteomic changes it induces. This integrated map will then be used to functionally test the necessity of prioritized nodes—such as specific autophagy adaptors or ER-associated degradation components—in cardiomyocytes derived from PLN-R14del patient iPSCs, establishing a robust, network-aware mechanistic model.

ideas.title: Adapting Single-Molecule Force Spectroscopy to Probe Single Sarcomere Mechanics in RBPMS KO Cardiomyocytes
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core methodology from single-molecule force spectroscopy in biophysics to the problem of measuring single sarcomere mechanics. In the analogous field, techniques like AFM-based force spectroscopy are used to probe the nanomechanics of individual protein complexes by specifically tethering the AFM tip to the molecule of interest. The adapted solution applies this principle to cardiomyocyte sarcomeres. An AFM tip is functionalized with an α-actinin antibody to achieve specific mechanical linkage to a single Z-disc in a permeabilized, adhered cardiomyocyte from an RBPMS knockout model. By performing precise force-extension measurements on this single, functionally isolated sarcomere, the experiment directly quantifies its intrinsic passive mechanical properties—such as resting tension, stiffness, and viscoelastic response—free from the confounding effects of neighboring sarcomeres and cellular cytoskeleton. This approach allows for a definitive test of whether RBPMS regulates cardiac mechanics at the most fundamental, scaled-down unit of contraction.

ideas.title: Optical Mapping-Guided Disaggregase Screening to Dissolve Arrhythmogenic Protein Clusters
ideas.description: This project integrates optical mapping of cardiac electrophysiology in RBPMS-deficient hearts with high-throughput disaggregase screening technology. The core innovation is to use optical mapping not just as an endpoint assay, but as a real-time, functional guide to identify which perinuclear protein aggregates (such as PLN clusters resulting from splicing defects) are directly responsible for observed arrhythmic phenotypes. Candidate disaggregase compounds, identified from neurodegenerative disease screens, are then applied, and their efficacy is quantified by the optical mapping system's ability to measure the simultaneous dissolution of aggregates and normalization of conduction velocity, action potential duration, and arrhythmia inducibility. This approach directly links molecular intervention to functional cardiac rescue, creating a targeted discovery pipeline for anti-arrhythmic therapies that resolve the underlying proteinopathy.

ideas.title: Context-Aware Mapping of Compensatory RBP Networks Following RBPMS Depletion
ideas.description: This project maps the landscape of RNA-binding protein (RBP) compensation for RBPMS loss across diverse cellular contexts to identify targets with robust therapeutic potential. Parallel, barcoded CRISPR-based synthetic lethal screens are conducted in isogenic cell models engineered to mimic different tissue lineages, disease states, and metabolic conditions, all upon inducible RBPMS knockout. By comparing genetic interaction networks across these contexts, the approach distinguishes universal, core synthetic lethal RBPs from context-specific ones, and reveals higher-order compensatory modules. This context-aware map is then validated in primary cells and relevant in vivo models to prioritize compensatory interactions most likely to be relevant in physiological and pathological settings.

ideas.title: A function-coupled disaggregase screen for PLN aggregates that preserves physiological regulation
ideas.description: This redesigned approach integrates phenotypic screening with immediate functional validation to overcome the critical flaw of therapeutic toxicity. It uses patient-derived or genetically engineered cardiomyocytes with pathogenic PLN aggregates to perform a high-throughput imaging screen for perinuclear cluster dissolution. Crucially, every hit from this primary screen is automatically subjected to a secondary assay measuring its impact on calcium transients and contractility. Only compounds that successfully clear aggregates while simultaneously restoring or preserving normal calcium handling and SERCA2a pump regulation are advanced, ensuring therapeutic relevance and safety.

ideas.title: Study DWORF in heterozygous PLN-R14del mice using a 'therapeutic window' dosing strategy adapted from oncology
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed by using an analogy from oncology. The analogous problem is treating cancers with heterozygous driver mutations using targeted therapies, where the solution is to find a drug dose that creates a 'therapeutic window'—inhibiting the mutant protein without completely ablating the essential function of the wild-type counterpart. Adapting this solution, the research would examine DWORF's benefit in heterozygous PLN-R14del mice not with a single, high-level overexpression, but by systematically testing a range of DWORF expression levels (e.g., via titratable AAV vectors or a dose-response study). The goal is to identify an optimal therapeutic dose that maximally counteracts the mutant PLN's inhibitory effect on SERCA2a while avoiding potential dysfunction from excessive DWORF activity on the remaining wild-type PLN, thereby defining a safe and effective gene therapy strategy for the more common heterozygous patient genotype.

ideas.title: Direct nanomechanical probing of PLN aggregates as the primary driver of sarcomere dysfunction
ideas.description: This research direction fuses single-sarcomere biomechanics with the hypothesis that phospholamban (PLN) aggregation is a primary disease driver. It proposes using atomic force microscopy on cardiomyocytes (including RBPMS KO and PLN mutation models) to measure the direct nanomechanical impact of PLN aggregates on sarcomere structure and force generation. This approach will test whether aggregates physically disrupt Z-disc or thin filament integrity, leading to mechanical dysfunction independent of, or prior to, measurable calcium handling defects. The synergy lies in directly linking a specific molecular pathology (aggregation) to its ultimate mechanical consequence at the smallest functional scale, validating it as a primary therapeutic target.

ideas.title: A CETSA-MS platform to identify direct RBPMS binders with functional validation in native contexts
ideas.description: This project implements a CETSA-MS primary screen to identify small molecules that induce thermal stabilization of the RBPMS protein in live cells, indicating direct target engagement. Hits from this unbiased binding screen are then rigorously validated in secondary assays that measure RBPMS's specific molecular functions—such as alternative splicing of endogenous targets and regulation of target mRNA stability—in physiologically relevant cell models. This approach ensures identified compounds directly interact with RBPMS or its functional complex and modulate its authentic biological activities, bypassing the artifacts of reporter-based systems. The final output is a set of high-confidence chemical probes that specifically enhance or inhibit RBPMS function through direct binding.

ideas.title: Adapting Longitudinal Cohort Studies from Epidemiology to Model Lifespan Effects of DWORF Overexpression
ideas.description: This idea adapts the longitudinal cohort study framework from epidemiology to investigate the long-term effects of DWORF on survival. In the analogous field, the multi-decade follow-up of human cohorts is used to determine how a protective factor influences lifespan and age-related morbidity. The adapted solution is to establish a defined murine cohort with DWORF overexpression and matched controls, subject them to a rigorous, pre-planned schedule of periodic cardiac and systemic functional assessments (e.g., echocardiography, biomarker panels, frailty scoring) at extended intervals beyond the initial 18-week point, continuing until natural death. The primary outcome would be lifespan, analyzed with survival statistics, while serial data would reveal the trajectory of cardiac function and the onset of age-related decline, providing a complete picture of DWORF's role in cardiac aging and longevity.

ideas.title: Applying Cryptographic Hash Collision Detection to Identify Non-Canonical RBPMS Binding Motifs
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that the CAC motif is the sole RBPMS recognition element by drawing an analogy from cryptography. In cryptography, finding hash collisions—different inputs producing the same output—reveals hidden vulnerabilities. The analogous solution involves systematic differential analysis to find these deviations from the expected one-to-one mapping. Adapted to this context, we treat the CAC-RBPMS binding as the primary expected 'input-output' pair. The research would then systematically search for 'collisions': instances where RBPMS binding and splicing regulation occur in the absence of a canonical CAC motif. By employing high-throughput comparative analyses (e.g., integrating enhanced CLIP-seq data with deep splicing phenotyping), the goal is to identify and characterize these non-canonical binding sites, which could be alternative sequence motifs, specific RNA secondary structures, or composite elements formed with co-factors, thereby expanding the understanding of RBPMS's regulatory code.

ideas.title: Post-Onset Therapeutic Window for DWORF Restoration in Heart Failure
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that intervention must be purely preventive by proposing a post-onset therapeutic window for DWORF restoration in heart failure. The analogy is drawn from neurology, specifically the established solution for ischemic stroke where a time-sensitive but post-symptom window exists to restore blood flow and salvage the ischemic penumbra. The adapted research idea is to test if a similar 'cardiac penumbra' or salvageable state exists after heart failure onset. By restoring DWORF expression at progressively later time points following the onset of symptomatic heart failure in an experimental model, the study aims to define the duration and efficacy of this therapeutic window, potentially expanding treatment options beyond pure prevention.

ideas.title: Apply a chemical biology screen for protein resolubilization, inspired by amyloid-targeting in prion disease, to PLN aggregates
ideas.description: This idea applies an analogous solution from prion and amyloid disease research to the problem of perinuclear PLN (phospholamban) aggregates. In prion research, high-throughput chemical screens using FRET, protein-fragment complementation, or amyloid-dye assays have successfully identified compounds that resolubilize or clear pathogenic protein aggregates like PrP^Sc. We adapt this methodological framework by developing a cell-based or in vitro high-throughput screen where pathological PLN aggregation is reported by a similar sensitive assay (e.g., FRET-tagged PLN monomers that signal upon cluster dissolution). Screening diverse compound libraries with this platform will aim to discover novel chemical entities that specifically dissolve the perinuclear PLN clusters, offering a potential therapeutic strategy for related cardiomyopathies.

ideas.title: Synthetic Lethal Network Mapping via Compensatory Circuit Analysis
ideas.description: This research adapts the circuit redundancy analysis approach from electrical engineering to solve the biological inverse problem of identifying compensatory RBPs for RBPMS loss. In circuit design, engineers systematically remove components and observe which parallel pathways maintain function through fault injection testing. Adapted to molecular biology, we propose to treat the RBP network as a biological circuit and perform systematic perturbation mapping: combine complete RBPMS knockout with graded, sub-lethal inhibition of other RBPs using degron systems or titrated RNAi. By measuring which perturbation combinations cause network collapse (via viability assays and transcriptomic profiling), we can identify not just synthetic lethal partners but map the hierarchy of compensatory pathways that maintain RNA regulatory function. This circuit-based approach reveals the redundancy architecture of the RBP network rather than just binary synthetic lethal interactions.
